To: aisha.rahman@redwood.ai
Cc: sara.jenkins@acme-hr.com
Date: Sun, 13 Sep 2026 08:27:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Invoice submission options + sample file for multi-PO envelopes

Hi Aisha (and team),

Thanks — we can do portal-first. A couple of clarifications from our side before we lock the format: 

- PO-less invoices: occasionally we invoice for non-PO services (e.g., host fees). We typically leave PO empty and include a "Billing Description" column. Will validation reject rows without PO#?
- Credit notes: do you want them sent as negative-line CSVs or separate PDF credit memos with a reference?
- Vendor ID: we do not have a Redwood vendor-id field in our ERP yet. We can include the wrapper filename pattern you suggested.

Attached: lumos_answers_2026-09-13.docx (answers & sample CSV generated by our ERP).

Quick ask: if we go email fallback, what is the SLA for acknowledgements? We ask because our treasury needs a confirmation for payment cycles.

Best,
Clara Munro
AP Lead, Lumos Supply
finance@lumos-supply.com



From: jordan.blake@redwood.ai
To: aisha.rahman@redwood.ai, monica.patel@redwood.ai, vanessa.ortiz@redwood.ai
Cc: finance@lumos-supply.com
Date: Mon, 14 Sep 2026 10:17:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Invoice submission options + sample file for multi-PO envelopes

Quick reply with procurement constraints and mapping guidance.

Team — from a procurement and payment ops perspective: portal-first is required for standard PO-matched invoices. Email fallback is acceptable for: vendor go-live exceptions, PO-less invoices, or when vendors cannot connect to the portal. We should make the fallback as deterministic as possible:

1) Subject token: exact match prefix must be "Inv-Redwood:" followed by vendor-id (we will assign and share). Examples: "Inv-Redwood: LUM-5532". Subject token simplifies auto-routing.

2) Envelope CSV rules (when sending multiple invoices in one email): we will treat the CSV as the source of truth for mapping. Minimal required columns: vendor_id, invoice_number, invoice_date, total_amount, po_number (can be blank), invoice_type (INVOICE|CREDIT|DEBIT), file_reference (filename of attached PDF). If file_reference missing, system will attempt to match by invoice_number.

3) PO-less rows: allow blank po_number but require invoice_type=INVOICE and a billing_description. Flag these for manual review — payment terms default to 60 days until a finance exception is approved.

4) Acknowledgements: for email fallback we will send automated ACK within 2 business hours confirming ingestion (or explicit failure reasons). Portal uploads have immediate UI acknowledgements.

I attached a tiny example CSV snippet inline here so Lumos can replicate formatting: 

---lumos_example.csv---
vendor_id,invoice_number,invoice_date,total_amount,po_number,invoice_type,file_reference,billing_description
LUM-5532,INV-2026-0912-01,2026-09-12,12500.00,PO-8892,INVOICE,INV-2026-0912-01.pdf,Monthly hosting fees

---

If Lumos cannot populate vendor_id yet, they must use the filename wrapper pattern and include the subject token.

Jordan Blake
Sr. Procurement Lead
Redwood Inference
jordan.blake@redwood.ai



From: aisha.rahman@redwood.ai
To: finance@lumos-supply.com (Lumos AP)
Cc: monica.patel@redwood.ai, vanessa.ortiz@redwood.ai, jordan.blake@redwood.ai
Date: Mon, 14 Sep 2026 16:22:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Invoice submission options + sample file for multi-PO envelopes

Clara — thanks for the quick answers and for the attached doc. Next steps and decisions we agreed on: 

- Onboard path: portal-first. Lumos will pilot portal uploads for standard PO-matched invoices starting next Tuesday (2026-09-21).
- Email fallback: Lumos will use subject token "Inv-Redwood: LUM-5532" for the pilot and the wrapper filename pattern lumos_LUM-5532_envelope_YYYYMMDD.csv until vendor_id is in their ERP.
- CSV envelope: Lumos will include the columns Jordan listed; Lumos team confirmed they can add invoice_type and file_reference.
- PO-less handling: will be allowed but set to pending review with 60-day payment terms unless finance exception is approved.
- ACK SLA: Redwood will send ingestion ACKs within 2 business hours for email fallback.

Action items: 
- Lumos (Clara): share a signed onboarding form. We have sent a DocuSign link for the vendor onboarding (see Drive link).
- Redwood (Monica): configure routing rule for subject token and add Lumos to the portal pilot group.

Thanks all — pleased we have a clean path.

Best,
Aisha Rahman
Manager, Procurement Ops
Redwood Inference
aisha.rahman@redwood.ai

Attachments: lumos_vendor_onboarding_form.pdf (signed copy expected), lumos_sample_invoice_envelope.csv (finalized fields)

Minimal required columns: vendor_id, invoice_number, invoice_date, total_amount, po_number (can be blank), invoice_type (INVOICE|CREDIT|DEBIT), file_reference (filename of attached PDF). If file_reference missing, system will attempt to match by invoice_number.

3) PO-less rows: allow blank po_number but require invoice_type=INVOICE and a billing_description. Flag these for manual review — payment terms default to 60 days until a finance exception is approved.

4) Acknowledgements: for email fallback we will send automated ACK within 2 business hours confirming ingestion (or explicit failure reasons). Portal uploads have immediate UI acknowledgements.

I attached a tiny example CSV snippet inline here so Lumos can replicate formatting: 

---lumos_example.csv---
vendor_id,invoice_number,invoice_date,total_amount,po_number,invoice_type,file_reference,billing_description
LUM-5532,INV-2026-0912-01,2026-09-12,12500.00,PO-8892,INVOICE,INV-2026-0912-01.pdf,Monthly hosting fees

---

If Lumos cannot populate vendor_id yet, they must use the filename wrapper pattern and include the subject token.

Jordan Blake

Redwood Inference

- Email fallback: Lumos will use subject token "Inv-Redwood: LUM-5532" for the pilot and the wrapper filename pattern lumos_LUM-5532_envelope_YYYYMMDD.csv until vendor_id is in their ERP.

Subject: Post-demo follow-up: privacy scope, data residency, and log retention

Hi Samantha / Oliver —

Thanks again for your time earlier. Quick recap and the follow-ups I promised: 

1) Scope question: you asked whether Redwood would be a Processor vs Controller for inference data collected during HealthSync's integration. Can you confirm if you expect Redwood to be named as a Processor in your DPA, or do you need a different mapping because you plan to send regulated patient identifiers?

2) Residency: you mentioned a hard requirement for EU residency for data at rest and processing. Current options: Hosted EU region (eu-west-1) or Dedicated (reserved capacity) deployed in your VPC. Which option would you prefer for the pilot?

3) Logging & retention: you asked what audit logs contain and our retention defaults. Attached is a small sample audit log extract and a short list of typical log fields (see attachment: sample_audit_logs.csv). For the pilot we can adjust retention — what target retention window do you need?

4) Subprocessors & breach notice: we maintain an up-to-date subprocessor list we can provide. Also noted: you requested guaranteed notification timeline for subprocessor incidents. Would 30-day pre-notice for new subprocessors and 48-hour initial notification for incidents be acceptable? Please flag if your legal needs something different.

Next steps I can take immediately:
- Share the current DPA with inline comments for your legal to review (attached: HealthSync_DPA_questions.pdf).
- Loop in Legal and Solutions to give short answers on processor/controller status and the feasibility of residency controls.

If you can reply with three quick items: preferred residency option, required log retention period, and whether you want Redwood named as Processor in DPA, I’ll get legal the exact redline asks.

Thanks — talk soon.

Best,
Ben Carter
Enterprise Account Executive
Redwood Inference
Attachments: HealthSync_DPA_questions.pdf (pdf), sample_audit_logs.csv (csv)

Subject: Re: Post-demo follow-up: privacy scope, data residency, and log retention

Hi Ben —

Thanks for the recap — that’s helpful. Answers / clarifications below: 

- Controller vs Processor: HealthSync expects Redwood to be a Processor for inference requests, but we need confirmation in writing about data access by Redwood employees (e.g., for debugging). Can you confirm if debug access is available and whether it’s restricted to specific roles with MFA and logging?

- Residency: EU residency is required for pilot data. eu-west-1 hosted region is acceptable if processing and backups are guaranteed to stay in-region. We’d prefer Dedicated if it simplifies compliance, but need a cost/lead time estimate.

- Logs & retention: We need audit logs that capture request metadata (timestamp, model/version, request size, requester id) and retention of 180 days minimum for the pilot. Can you confirm fields and whether we can export logs to our SIEM?

- Subprocessors & breach: please provide the current subprocessor list and your standard breach-notification SLA. Also please confirm whether Redwood will notify us if a subprocessor experiences a security event that may affect our data and how quickly.

Finally — we will need SOC 2 Type II and ISO27001 evidence for procurement. If you can send a redline DPA and short answers to the above, we can move to legal review.

Appreciate it,
Samantha Lee

From: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.ai>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>, Samantha Lee <samantha.lee@healthsync.ai>
Cc: Vivek Kulkarni <vivek.kulkarni@redwood.ai>, Oliver Grant <oliver.grant@medboard.com>
Date: Tue, 14 Sep 2026 11:03:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Post-demo follow-up: privacy scope, data residency, and log retention

Hi all —

Thanks for the clear questions — quick responses and proposed next steps below. Vivek and I have reviewed and attached a redline DPA that addresses the pilot ask.

1) Processor vs Controller: Redwood acts as a Processor for inference requests by default. We will process data strictly on behalf of HealthSync per the DPA language; Redwood employees do not have routine access to customer inference payloads. Exceptions (e.g., debugging access) are narrow, logged, and require explicit approval. We will add a clause to the DPA that documents role-based access controls, MFA for privileged access, and an auditable approval workflow.

2) Residency: Hosted eu-west-1 keeps data and backups in-region. For customers seeking additional assurance we recommend Dedicated in-customer VPC. Estimated lead time for a Dedicated EU pilot is ~4–6 weeks; rough incremental monthly cost estimate provided by Solutions (Vivek) in the attachment.

3) Logs & retention: Default retention is 180 days. Audit logs include timestamp, model_id, model_version, request_id (correlated to your requester id), token counts, originating region, and anonymized caller metadata. Logs can be exported via S3 export or pushed to your SIEM via secure delivery; we'll add export instructions to the playbook. We can commit to 180 days retention for the pilot and provide a one-time export of historical logs if needed.

4) Subprocessors & breach notice: We will provide a current subprocessor list (attached). Redwood’s standard process is: 30 days advance notice for new subprocessors when feasible, and initial incident notification to customers within 48 hours of detection of an event that materially affects customer data plus a follow-up report. We’ll include a subprocessor notification clause in the redline.

Next steps / deliverables: 
- I’m attaching our redline: Redwood_DPA_Redline_20260914.pdf. Please review and let us know any required edits by 2026-09-16 so we can get Legal sign-off.
- Vivek will follow up (cc’d) with a short tech note showing the sample log fields and a cost/lead-time estimate for Dedicated EU.
- We’ll also share SOC2 evidence via the secure link in the related_links above.

If that timeline works, we can start contract review and provision a sandbox EU project for the pilot.

Regards,
Marissa Cole
Legal Operations, Redwood Inference
Attachments: Redwood_DPA_Redline_20260914.pdf (pdf)

Cc: Vivek Kulkarni <vivek.kulkarni@redwood.ai>, Oliver Grant <oliver.grant@medboard.com>
Date: Tue, 14 Sep 2026 11:03:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Post-demo follow-up: privacy scope, data residency, and log retention

Hi all —

Thanks for the clear questions — quick responses and proposed next steps below. Vivek and I have reviewed and attached a redline DPA that addresses the pilot ask.

1) Processor vs Controller: Redwood acts as a Processor for inference requests by default. We will process data strictly on behalf of HealthSync per the DPA language; Redwood employees do not have routine access to customer inference payloads. Exceptions (e.g., debugging access) are narrow, logged, and require explicit approval. We will add a clause to the DPA that documents role-based access controls, MFA for privileged access, and an auditable approval workflow.

2) Residency: Hosted eu-west-1 keeps data and backups in-region. For customers seeking additional assurance we recommend Dedicated in-customer VPC. Estimated lead time for a Dedicated EU pilot is ~4–6 weeks; rough incremental monthly cost estimate provided by Solutions (Vivek) in the attachment.

3) Logs & retention: Default retention is 180 days. Audit logs include timestamp, model_id, model_version, request_id (correlated to your requester id), token counts, originating region, and anonymized caller metadata. Logs can be exported via S3 export or pushed to your SIEM via secure delivery; we'll add export instructions to the playbook. We can commit to 180 days retention for the pilot and provide a one-time export of historical logs if needed.

4) Subprocessors & breach notice: We will provide a current subprocessor list (attached). Redwood’s standard process is: 30 days advance notice for new subprocessors when feasible, and initial incident notification to customers within 48 hours of detection of an event that materially affects customer data plus a follow-up report. We’ll include a subprocessor notification clause in the redline.

Next steps / deliverables: 
- I’m attaching our redline: Redwood_DPA_Redline_20260914.pdf. Please review and let us know any required edits by 2026-09-16 so we can get Legal sign-off.
- Vivek will follow up (cc’d) with a short tech note showing the sample log fields and a cost/lead-time estimate for Dedicated EU.
- We’ll also share SOC2 evidence via the secure link in the related_links above.

If that timeline works, we can start contract review and provision a sandbox EU project for the pilot.

Regards,
Marissa Cole
Legal Operations, Redwood Inference
Attachments: Redwood_DPA_Redline_20260914.pdf (pdf)

title: Re: Production p99 latency spikes — remediation & concession request
preview:
From: Laura Jenkins <laura.jenkins@finovate.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: procurement@finovate.com
Date: Tue, 14 Sep 2026 09:12:00 -0400
Subject: Production p99 latency spikes — impact to customers

Ava —

Over the last 72 hours we've seen repeated p99 tail-latency spikes on the Payments normalization route. Two downstream microservices timed out yesterday during our evening peak window and we had to roll traffic to a cached fallback, which degraded authorization accuracy for a short period. Our ops team attached a short CSV with the latency snapshots and a timeline.

This is impacting three of our largest merchants and we've been asked for a remediation plan + commercial offset while you investigate. Our renewals committee is watching this closely — we need a clear proposed concession (credit / free month / remediation commitments) and a timeline for the fix.

Please advise next steps and an ETA for a remediation mitigation we can communicate to our customers.

Attachments: finovate_p99_snapshot_sep14.csv (12KB)

Thanks,
Laura Jenkins
Head of Platform Reliability
Finovate Payments
laura.jenkins@finovate.com


From: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwoodinference.com>
To: Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwoodinference.com>, Naomi Feldman <naomi.feldman@redwoodinference.com>, Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Tue, 14 Sep 2026 10:05:00 -0400
Subject: Fwd: Production p99 latency spikes — impact to customers

Forwarding Laura's note — Finovate reports sustained tail-latency during evening peaks (attached). They explicitly asked for a remediation plan + commercial offset.

My read: the immediate ask is two-fold: 1) a commercial concession to restore trust (credit or free month) and 2) operational mitigation to stop recurrence (routing change / dedicated capacity window).

Proposed quick structure for exec review:
- Immediate one-month service credit applied to next invoice (covers customer pain window).
- Pro-rated usage credit for any overage during the incident days (auto-offset).
- Offer a 90-day dedicated capacity reservation for their peak window (we staff and pin nodes, limited to their busiest 4 hours/day) at a 25% discount; trialable and reversible.
- Condition: Finovate signs an amendment with limited waiver language covering historical incidents and accepting standard reversion terms.

I attached a draft concession term sheet (proposed_concession_term.docx). Will need legal to confirm wording and finance to confirm math. Karthik — this is renewal-risky; any commercial input? Marcus — any legal/contract constraints we should be aware of for an ad-hoc credit + capacity promise?

Attachments: proposed_concession_term.docx

Thanks,
Ava Chen
Account Lead — Strategic Accounts
Redwood Inference


From: Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwoodinference.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwoodinference.com>, Naomi Feldman <naomi.feldman@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Tue, 14 Sep 2026 13:40:00 -0400
Subject: Re: Fwd: Production p99 latency spikes — impact to customers

+1 on Ava's suggested approach. From a commercial perspective:
- Finovate is in late-stage renewal discussions and this is the biggest immediate risk to close.
- A one-month unconditional credit plus the pro-rated offset is the minimum to stabilize the relationship; we should avoid an open-ended blanket concession.
- The dedicated-capacity trial is a strong retention lever (keeps them on Redwood vs. a competitor offering reserved capacity).

Ask for exec approval on the financial cap: recommend capping the concession at $150k total (credit + trial discount) unless leadership signs off on higher. If we can get Legal and Finance to pre-authorize the amendment, I can present to Finovate's renewal committee by EOD tomorrow.

Naomi — please prepare a customer-facing mitigation note summarizing what we'll do operationally and the expected timeline for eliminating the tail spikes. Marcus — if Legal can draft a short amendment with waiver + limited liability language, Karthik needs that to close the loop.

Karthik
SVP, Sales
Redwood Inference


From: Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwoodinference.com>
To: Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwoodinference.com>, Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwoodinference.com>, Naomi Feldman <naomi.feldman@naomi.feldman@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Tue, 14 Sep 2026 16:10:00 -0400
Subject: Re: Fwd: Production p99 latency spikes — impact to customers

I've reviewed the draft concession term sheet. Key legal points / redlines:
1) Credit mechanics: specify that credits apply to future invoices and are non-refundable; include a $150k cap unless signed off by CFO.
2) Trial capacity: call this a "reserved-capacity pilot" for 90 days; include a clause for termination with 7 days' notice and SLA measurement windows.
3) Waiver language: limited release for the incidents described and a carve-out for negligence or gross misconduct — we cannot accept an unlimited indemnity.
4) Signature flow: amendments executed via DocuSign; include an explicit acceptance checkbox for the concession and pilot terms.

I've uploaded a redlined doc to the internal drive and created a DocuSign placeholder link for the amendment. If Sales confirms the $150k cap and Finance greenlights the accounting treatment, Legal is ok to proceed.

Related: please run final numbers on the proposed cap vs. expected churn economics.

Marcus Lin
Senior Counsel, Contracts
Redwood Inference


From: Naomi Feldman <naomi.feldman@redwoodinference.com>
To: Laura Jenkins <laura.jenkins@finovate.com>, Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwoodinference.com>, Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwoodinference.com>, procurement@finovate.com, Carlos Ruiz <c.ruiz@finovate.com>
Date: Tue, 14 Sep 2026 18:02:00 -0400
Subject: Final: Concession & remediation plan — next steps

Laura / Finovate team — thanks for raising this and for the data you shared. We've reviewed the incident and coordinated across Sales, Legal, and Engineering. This is the remediation and concession package Redwood will extend: 

1) Commercial concession
- One (1) month service credit applied to Finovate's next invoice (non-refundable).
- Pro-rated usage credit for the incident days (applied as invoice offset).
- Total concession capped at $150,000 unless mutually agreed in writing by Redwood's CFO.

2) Operational remediation
- Redwood will provision a 90-day reserved-capacity pilot for Finovate's 18:00–22:00 ET peak window. The pilot will include dedicated nodes, monitoring runbooks, and priority ops escalation.
- Engineering will run traffic-shaping rules and increased KV cache retention for the pilot window; we will provide a mitigation playbook within 48 hours.

3) Governance & sign-off
- We will deliver a short amendment via DocuSign for signature (link below). The amendment contains the credit mechanics, pilot terms, and a limited release for the incidents cited.
- Once signed, credits will be applied to the next billing cycle and the pilot will be scheduled within 7 business days.

DocuSign amendment (placeholder): https://docusign.com/placeholder/ABC123-AMEND
Internal deal link: https://hubspot.redwoodinference.com/deal/DEAL-13472

Please confirm acceptance or propose edits. Karthik: I'll coordinate the customer presentation once we have the signed amendment. Marcus: thanks for the redlines; we accept the $150k cap and the waiver language as discussed.

Laura — thanks again. Hoping this reestablishes confidence with your ops + renewals teams.

Best,
Naomi Feldman
Head of Customer Success
Redwood Inference

title: Dedicated capacity sizing follow-up — VertoHealth throughput & GPU preferences
preview:
From: Ben Carter <ben@redwoodinference.com>
To: Rebecca Lin <rebecca@vertohealth.com>, procurement@vertohealth.com
Cc: Karthik Iyer <karthik@redwoodinference.com>, Priyom Das <priyom@redwoodinference.com>
Date: 2026-09-12T16:05:00-07:00
Subject: Dedicated capacity sizing follow-up — VertoHealth throughput & GPU preferences

Hi Rebecca — thanks again for the call earlier. Quick recap and the asks we discussed so the team can size a dedicated pool that meets your SLA targets. I pasted the high-level bullets below and attached a working capacity estimate (verto-capacity-estimate-v1.xlsx) and a sample token mix export.

High-level notes from today:
- Use case: near-real-time clinical note classification + short generation snippets for care coordination. Mix: 70% classification (short inputs, ~100 tokens), 30% generation (avg 450 tokens output).
- Traffic pattern: peak 8–10AM and 5–7PM PT, with steady background of ~150 TPS and peaks up to ~650 TPS for short classification requests. Generation requests are bursty (peak ~190 TPS) but have longer server-side token counts.
- Latency target: p95 inference <= 120 ms for classification; generation p95 <= 650 ms.
- Data residency: US-only, VPC preferred. Private on-prem considered for compliance but higher friction.

Questions / asks for next steps (so we can finalize a Dedicated SKU recommendation):
1) Confirm the token mix per request type (we used 70/30 above) and an estimated average output token length for generation (we assumed 450).
2) Are you open to 8-bit quantized model variants (faster, cheaper) or do you require full FP16 for fidelity?
3) Any hard constraints on model family? (e.g., prefer Llama-2/Redwood-tuned, or do you need a specific partner model)
4) Do you expect sustained background TPS >100 or is most of it burst driven?

Proposed immediate next steps:
- We’ll iterate the attached capacity worksheet with GPU options (A100 80GB, H100, and NTX-like options) and produce 2 CAPEX/commit scenarios: conservative (reserve for 99th pct) and flexible (autoscale + burst).
- Karthik will run a quick perf model mapping based on your token distributions and return projected p95 latencies per GPU family.

I put a preliminary cost/throughput sheet in the attachment. If you can confirm token averages and whether 8-bit is acceptable, we’ll produce a recommended Dedicated configuration and a short runbook for failover to Hosted API for bursts.

Thanks —
Ben
Ben Carter
Senior Account Executive | Redwood Inference
ben@redwoodinference.com
Attachment(s): verto-capacity-estimate-v1.xlsx (spreadsheet), token_mix_sample.txt (text)

From: Rebecca Lin <rebecca@vertohealth.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: procurement@vertohealth.com
Date: 2026-09-12T18:34:00-07:00
Subject: Re: Dedicated capacity sizing follow-up — VertoHealth throughput & GPU preferences

Ben — thanks for the summary, that matches our internal notes. Quick answers inline: 

- Token mix: your 70/30 split is accurate for our initial roll-out cohort. For generation average output, we can tighten to 380–420 tokens (we’ll use 400 as a working avg).
- Quantization: clinical quality is a must for generation. We are OK testing 8-bit for classification paths, but generation should start FP16. If we see regressions we can tighten.
- Model family: we prefer Redwood-verified open models (where you can guarantee quantization profiles). Not locked to a single vendor model but must be supported in private VPC.
- Traffic profile: steady baseline is ~140 TPS (classification) and generation is near 0 outside office hours, with bursts during the two windows. Peaks you quoted are accurate.

Also procurement asked for the deal_id and a ballpark monthly committed spend range so they can prep internal approvals. Do you have a conservative vs flexible number?

Rebecca
Rebecca Lin
Director, Product — VertoHealth
rebecca@vertohealth.com

From: Ben Carter <ben@redwoodinference.com>
To: Rebecca Lin <rebecca@vertohealth.com>
Cc: Karthik Iyer <karthik@redwoodinference.com>, Priyom Das <priyom@redwoodinference.com>, procurement@vertohealth.com
Date: 2026-09-12T19:12:00-07:00
Subject: Re: Dedicated capacity sizing follow-up — VertoHealth throughput & GPU preferences

Thanks, Rebecca — super helpful. We'll use 70/30 with generation avg = 400 tokens and plan for:
- Classification path: allow 8-bit quantization (much lower cost)
- Generation path: FP16 for first rollout, with an experiment to test 8-bit delta later in a non-production slot.

Immediate deliverables by EOD Tuesday (2026-09-14):
- Updated capacity worksheet with three GPU scenarios (H100, A100 x2, and an efficient inference-oriented instance) and estimated committed monthly spend ranges for conservative vs flexible models.
- A short appendix that shows projected p95 latencies given your token mix per GPU family (Karthik will run this).
- A draft mutual action plan for procurement (deal ID VH-2026-09-8421) with approval checkpoints and a 30/60/90 rollout milestone.

Priyom — can you draft the mutual action plan and include an itemized list for the procurement team (contract milestones, SOC2 evidence, VPC checklist)? Karthik — can you run perf mapping and include recommended concurrency settings and expected TPS per GPU under our assumed batch/config?

Ben

On Sep 12, 2026, at 16:05, Ben Carter <ben@redwoodinference.com> wrote:
> (original message above)


From: Karthik Iyer <karthik@redwoodinference.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben@redwoodinference.com>, Priyom Das <priyom@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Rebecca Lin <rebecca@vertohealth.com>, procurement@vertohealth.com
Date: 2026-09-13T09:28:00-07:00
Subject: Re: Dedicated capacity sizing follow-up — VertoHealth throughput & GPU preferences

Ben / Priyom — quick status update on the perf-side; I ran a back-of-envelope with the token distribution Rebecca confirmed. Findings: 

- Classification (avg 100 tokens): on A100 80GB we can sustain ~850 concurrent classification TPS with p95 < 100 ms using batch size 64 and continuous batching. H100 improves headroom ~1.4x at similar configs. 8-bit quant gives ~20–30% extra throughput for classification.
- Generation (avg 400 output + 50 prompt) => ~450 tokens total: A100 single GPU sustained throughput for FP16 generation with concurrency tuned for latency is roughly 18–24 TPS p95 ~450–550 ms depending on temperature/decoding strategy. H100 pushes that to 28–34 TPS under same latency. Efficient inference instances (quantized kernels) can get to ~40 TPS for generation in 8-bit but Rebecca flagged generation should be FP16 initially.

Sizing suggestion (starting point):
- Conservative committed pool: 4 x H100 (mix for gen + classification) — supports target peaks with headroom and predictable latency.
- Cost-efficient hybrid: 2 x H100 (for generation) + autoscaling classification fleet (A100 or quantized nodes) that handles bursts. Fallback to Hosted API for overflow temp bursts.

Concurrency notes for the runbook: set generation concurrency per model to 12–16 active requests per GPU (this keeps p95 under 650 ms with our decoding profile). Classification can be set high (100+ concurrent) if using batching and 8-bit.

I’ll assemble a short table for the capacity worksheet with expected TPS per GPU type and recommended concurrency settings. Priyom — I can send numbers to slot into the procurement cost table once Ben has the ask.

Karthik
Principal Performance Engineer | Redwood Inference

Attachment(s): perf-mapping-notes.txt (summary)

title: Observability export: Splunk HEC + on‑prem SIEM constraints and tracing hooks
preview:
From: Samantha Liu <samantha.liu@novapay.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Novapay SecOps <secops@novapay.com>
Date: Wed, 9 Sep 2026 14:05:00 -0700
Subject: Observability export constraints for on‑prem deployment

Hi Ava,

Thanks again for the walkthrough earlier. We’re starting the internal design for integrating Redwood Private (VPC deployment) with our on‑prem observability stack and ran into a few constraints I wanted to call out and get your recommended approach for: 

- Network: Our environment only allows outbound egress to a short, pre‑approved IP range (see attached novapay-allowed-egress-ips.csv). We cannot open arbitrary external endpoints.
- Auth: Everything must be mTLS with client certs or short‑lived tokens issued by our internal broker. We can avoid long‑lived shared tokens.
- Data residency: Logs/metrics/traces containing PII must be redacted before leaving our VPC; we prefer an in‑customer forwarder that sanitizes and pushes to our Splunk HEC cluster.
- Buffering: We cannot depend on continuous internet connectivity — need an on‑prem buffer/queue local to the VPC so data isn't lost during transient outages.
- Tracing: We want to capture Redwood trace IDs to correlate with our service traces. How do you recommend we surface trace hooks from the Private runtime (span/pseudospan exports)?

Can you outline the minimal architecture you recommend (sidecar vs privileged forwarder vs central relay), sample auth flows, and any limitations (rate limits, max batch sizes, TLS cipher requirements)? We’d like to run a small POC and a canary for one service first.

Attachment: novapay-allowed-egress-ips.csv (text/csv)

Thanks,
Samantha Liu
Director, Platform Security
Novapay



From: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwoodinference.com>
To: Samantha Liu <samantha.liu@novapay.com>
Cc: Novapay SecOps <secops@novapay.com>, Kimberly Park <kimberly.park@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Wed, 9 Sep 2026 17:18:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Observability export constraints for on‑prem deployment

Samantha — thanks for the clear constraints and the allowed IP list. Below is a concise proposal tailored to your rules, plus a few follow ups so we can scope the POC.

Proposal (high level)
- Deployment pattern: customer‑managed forwarder (lightweight process/sidecar) + optional central relay inside customer VPC.
  - Sidecar: deployed alongside Redwood inference nodes (recommended for strict per‑host redaction). Responsibilities: log/metric/trace collection, PII redaction rules, local buffering to disk (capped), and push to customer relay/HEC.
  - Central relay: a HA pair (VMs or k8s service) that receives sidecar traffic over mTLS, performs batching and rates, and forwards to your Splunk HEC cluster over the allowed egress IPs. This keeps per‑host ops light and centralizes HEC auth.

- Auth & transport:
  - mTLS between sidecars and central relay using customer‑issued client certificates (rotated via your PKI).
  - Between relay and Splunk HEC: mutual TLS + HEC tokens. If you prefer ephemeral tokens, we can support short‑lived signed tokens (Redwood supports validating token expiry).

- Buffering and durability:
  - Sidecar maintains write‑ahead log (disk ring buffer) with configurable retention and backpressure.
  - Relay persists into a local queue (filesystem or Kafka if you want durability beyond single instance).
  - When connectivity to external Splunk drops, relay will retry with exponential backoff and apply backpressure to sidecars.

- Redaction & schema mapping:
  - We’ll provide a redact hook library (the sidecar can run a small Lua/regex pipeline or call your redact service).
  - We’ll provide a field mapping template to convert Redwood runtime logs/traces to your Splunk HEC sourcetypes. See attached splunk-hec-proxy-sample.conf for examples.

- Tracing hooks:
  - The Private runtime can emit an outbound trace export in two flavors: 1) span samples as JSON over HTTPS, 2) lightweight trace references (traceID, spanID, minimal tags) for high‑QPS paths.
  - For correlation we recommend the lightweight reference mode forwarded to the relay; the relay enriches/joins using a short lived index. 
  - We’ve attached tracehook-sample.yaml with recommended sampling and tag filters. Note: full span export increases bandwidth significantly.

Questions / items we need from you before the POC
1) Confirm the IPs from your CSV that will be used for egress and whether NAT IPs are stable.
2) Confirm whether you want sidecar redaction (per‑host) or central redaction (relay). Sidecar is better for strict PII controls.
3) Rate expectations for logs/metrics/traces (events/sec and average payload size) so we can size relay/buffer.

POC plan (proposed)
- Week 0: Cert/key exchange and firewall allowlist update (we provide CSR templates).
- Week 1: Deploy sidecar on two nodes + small relay in staging. Run synthetic traffic and validate mTLS handshake and egress to your Splunk staging HEC.
- Week 2: Canary production service (single service) for 48 hours. Collect metrics and adjust sampling.

Attachments: splunk-hec-proxy-sample.conf (text/plain), tracehook-sample.yaml (text/yaml)

If this looks good I’ll loop in Diego from infra engineering to help with the sample config and Jonas from runtime to confirm trace export limits.

Best,
Ava Chen
Sr. Solutions Engineer — Private Deployments
Redwood Inference



From: Samantha Liu <samantha.liu@novapay.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Novapay SecOps <secops@novapay.com>
Date: Thu, 10 Sep 2026 08:40:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Observability export constraints for on‑prem deployment

Ava — this is great, thanks. A few clarifications: 

- Egress IPs: The CSV is accurate; NAT IPs are stable for the next quarter but we’ll request longer‑term allocations for production.
- Redaction: We prefer sidecar redaction to avoid raw PII leaving any host.
- Rate: Initial service will be ~450 events/s average, peaks ~2.5k events/s; average event ~1.2 KB. Traces: ~350 trace refs/s, full spans only for sampled errors (~1% of traces).
- Cipher policy: We require TLS1.2+ with AES128/256 GCM or CHACHA20_POLY1305; client certs signed by our internal PKI.

Can we get an ETA on the CSR template and a sample mTLS handshake test we can run? Also prefer the POC early next week — does your team have capacity Monday/Tuesday?

Thanks,
Samantha



From: Kimberly Park <kimberly.park@redwoodinference.com>
To: Samantha Liu <samantha.liu@novapay.com>, Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Diego Fernandez <diego.fernandez@redwoodinference.com>, Jonas Weber <jonas.weber@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Fri, 12 Sep 2026 10:22:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Observability export constraints for on‑prem deployment

Samantha — we can support Monday POC start. Quick plan + owners: 

1) Cert exchange and handshake test (owner: Diego) — we’ll send CSR templates and a small Go test binary that attempts an mTLS handshake to your relay IPs. Deliverable: success/fail log + TLS cipher list.
2) Sidecar build + config (owner: Jonas) — sidecar binary with redact hook enabled, default retention 24h, ring buffer 10GB for POC. We’ll supply splunk config and a dry‑run mode that prints batch payloads rather than posting.
3) Relay sizing + smoke tests (owner: Ava) — HA relay (2x small VMs), default batch window 5s, max batch 200 events, retry backoff 30s -> 5m. We’ll benchmark at 3k EPS to verify headroom.
4) Observability and rollbacks (owner: Kimberly) — we’ll provide dashboards (latency, queue depth, drop rate) and a rollback playbook.

Next steps:
- Diego will share CSR template and handshake binary by EOD today.
- Samantha: please share a staging HEC endpoint (FQDN/IP + port) and a test cert we can use for the handshake.
- We’ll open JIRA-OPS-1342 and put the POC checklist in the linked Drive doc.

Attachments referenced in thread: splunk-hec-proxy-sample.conf, tracehook-sample.yaml, novapay-allowed-egress-ips.csv

Kimberly Park
Head of Private Deployments — Customer Engineering
Redwood Inference

title: Dedicated throughput commitment & GPU pool recommendation
preview:
From: Sara Tan <sara.tan@greenwave.ai>
To: Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.ai>
Cc: Marco Ruiz <marco.ruiz@greenwave.ai>
Date: Tue, 7 Sep 2026 09:12:00 -0700
Subject: Inquiry: Dedicated capacity + throughput SLAs for inference

Hey Ben,

We ran your quickstart with a small pilot and are ready to explore a Dedicated deployment. Two asks from procurement/architecture:

- Ballpark committed monthly spend and available discount tiers for a 12-month reserved commit.
- Suggested GPU pool size for our expected workload (see quick numbers below).
- Any latency/throughput guarantees you can commit to (p99/P95) for production traffic.

Workload snapshot (avg day):
- User-facing chat: ~180k requests/day, avg 1.1 prompts per session.
- Avg input length: 350 tokens; avg generation: 280 tokens (so ~630 tokens total/request).
- Peak concurrency: roughly 180 concurrent requests during US business hours.
- Target p95 latency: <350 ms for streaming-first-token, or acceptable fallbacks if higher.

Attached is a short CSV with hourly pattern for a couple of sample days (greenwave_workload_report.csv).

Would love a recommended pool configuration and an outline of pricing/discounts so I can review with procurement.

Thanks,
Sara
Sr. Product Ops, GreenWave AI



From: Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.ai>
To: Sara Tan <sara.tan@greenwave.ai>
Cc: Laura Bennett <laura_bennett@redwood.ai>
Date: Tue, 7 Sep 2026 11:04:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Inquiry: Dedicated capacity + throughput SLAs for inference

Hi Sara — thanks for the data, this is exactly what we need to size a Dedicated pool. Looping in Laura (Solutions) to provide the GPU sizing and latency expectations. A few quick clarifying questions while we model cost:

1) Do you expect the 180k requests/day to be steady across the week or concentrated weekdays?
2) Are you ok with a model variant in 8-bit quantized form (we repeatedly see ~2-2.5x throughput improvement) or do you need FP16/FP32 for quality?
3) Any hard SLA requirements beyond p95/p99 for first-token?

I'll get you an initial recommended pool + commit tiers today.

Ben
Account Executive | Redwood Inference


From: Laura Bennett <laura_bennett@redwood.ai>
To: Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.ai>, Sara Tan <sara.tan@greenwave.ai>
Cc: Rishi Malhotra <rishi_malhotra@redwood.ai>
Date: Tue, 7 Sep 2026 14:28:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Inquiry: Dedicated capacity + throughput SLAs for inference

Sara — thanks for the workload CSV, that made this straightforward. Based on the numbers and conservative headroom for bursts, here are our recommendations and assumptions: 

Assumptions:
- Model: redwood-large-35B quantized to 8-bit (best quality/perf tradeoff). If you require full FP16, multiply GPUs by ~1.6x.
- Typical throughput per 80GB GPU (8-bit): ~220 tokens/sec for generation at your average context on our optimized runtime; sustained prefix+generation combined estimate ~140 requests/sec per GPU (at ~630 token total/request).
- We size for peak concurrency + 25% headroom.

Sizing (recommended):
- Peak concurrency target: 180 concurrent requests -> size calculation: 180 / 140 ≈ 1.3 GPUs. Add 25% headroom and routing/resilience -> recommend 2 GPUs minimum for baseline.
- Production recommendation (for redundancy, rolling upgrades, autoscaling margins, and expected growth): 6 x 80GB GPUs in a pool. That yields ~840 req/sec sustained capacity, handles spikes, and allows room for model upgrades and other tenants if you run multiple model variants.

Throughput & latency guidance:
- With 6 GPUs and our autoscaler configuration, expected p95 first-token latency: ~220–320 ms (dependent on network RTT and client batching). p99: ~380–520 ms during highest spikes, with graceful degradation paths (fallback to smaller quantized variants or cached prefix responses).

Committed tiers & pricing (ballpark):
- Monthly reserved commit options (12-month):
  * Small: 2 GPUs equivalent - $XX,XXX/mo (no discount)
  * Standard: 6 GPUs equivalent - $YY,YYY/mo (~15% discount off on-demand hosted rate)
  * Enterprise: 12+ GPUs - custom pricing (25–35% discount + prioritized capacity)
- We can align SLA windows on the Standard and Enterprise tiers (SLA-backed uptime and latency targets).

Next steps I propose:
1) If you confirm the model fidelity (8-bit ok?), we will run a short perf validation with your exact prompt set on a 2-GPU pilot to validate tokens/sec.
2) Ben will surface a proposal with the Standard (6 GPU) commit price and a draft SOW.
3) If procurement wants deeper discounts, share target monthly commit number and I’ll route to our pricing committee.

Attaching a quick sketch SOW and an example throughput worksheet (SOW_GW-4821_v1.pdf).

—Laura
Solutions Architect | Redwood Inference


From: Marco Ruiz <marco.ruiz@greenwave.ai>
To: Laura Bennett <laura_bennett@redwood.ai>, Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.ai>, Sara Tan <sara.tan@greenwave.ai>
Date: Wed, 8 Sep 2026 09:41:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Inquiry: Dedicated capacity + throughput SLAs for inference

Thanks Laura — couple clarifications from our infra/procurement:
- 8-bit quant is acceptable if there is a quick A/B validation to confirm user-visible quality differences are <2% on our metric.
- For discount evaluation: procurement is looking for a baseline committed spend of at least $75k/mo to trigger the mid-tier discount. Can you indicate the exact dollar ranges for the Small/Standard tiers?
- Also, will the 6 GPU pool be single-az or multi-az? We prefer multi-az for resilience.

Appreciate it — Marco
Head of Platform Engineering, GreenWave AI



From: Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.ai>
To: Marco Ruiz <marco.ruiz@greenwave.ai>, Laura Bennett <laura_bennett@redwood.ai>, Sara Tan <sara.tan@greenwave.ai>
Date: Thu, 9 Sep 2026 10:15:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Inquiry: Dedicated capacity + throughput SLAs for inference

Marco — good questions. Quick answers: 
- Dollar ranges (standardized example):
  * 2-GPU reserved commit: $32,500/mo
  * 6-GPU reserved commit (Standard): $72,000/mo — this maps to the mid-tier discount threshold and is what we recommend for production.
  * 12-GPU commit: $132,000/mo — enterprise tier for larger scale and dedicated priority routing.
- Multi-AZ: yes, we can provision the pool across two AZs inside your region (adds cross-AZ replication overhead; we included that in the Standard pricing above).
- SLA: Standard (6-GPU) includes a 99.5% uptime SLA and p95 latency objective; if you need stricter latency SLOs we can add a latency credit/penalty mechanism in the contract.

Laura — once you confirm the pricing line items I'll turn this into a short commercial proposal and SOW for signature.

Ben


From: Sara Tan <sara.tan@greenwave.ai>
To: Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.ai>, Marco Ruiz <marco.ruiz@greenwave.ai>
Cc: Laura Bennett <laura_bennett@redwood.ai>, Rishi Malhotra <rishi_malhotra@redwood.ai>
Date: Thu, 10 Sep 2026 14:03:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Inquiry: Dedicated capacity + throughput SLAs for inference

Sounds good — thanks for the clear options. Procurement is happy to start with the 6-GPU reserved commit at $72k/mo (12-month). Two quick items before we go to signature:

1) Please run a perf validation on our sample prompt set on a 2-GPU pilot (we want to confirm the A/B quality delta and validate tokens/sec).
2) Add a clause for an optional mid-term scale (after month 6) to expand to 12 GPUs at a pre-agreed uplift (we'll want the price protection).

If you can send the commercial proposal/SOW with those items, we'll route it internally next week.

Best,
Sara

title: Delta-tier overage & rebate framework
preview:
From: Alice Chen <alice.chen@acme-analytics.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.inference> , Lauren Bishop <lauren@redwood.inference>
Date: Tue, 1 Sep 2026 15:12:00 -0700
Subject: Delta-tier overage & rebate framework

Hi Ben / Lauren,

Thanks again for the demo last week — the latency profile and autoscaling looked promising for our analytics ingestion spikes. Before we take this to procurement I need to understand how Redwood would handle our usage pattern: big weekly spikes for model re-ranking followed by low baseline traffic.

Specifically we are trying to model:
- A 6-month pilot with a small commit (~$6k/mo) and large bursts (up to 10x) in launch months
- A desire for a cost safety mechanism: we are ok with overage rates but would like some rebate/true-up if monthly spikes are one-offs vs sustained growth

Can you share a recommended structure for: minimum monthly commitment, on-demand overage rates by band, and any rebate mechanics for one-time overages (quarterly true-up or delta-style rebates)? I attached our rough forecast (acme_forecast_q3.csv).

Procurement will want simple examples (numbers) and a clear true-up cadence.

Best,
Alice
Alice Chen
Head of Platform
Acme Analytics


From: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.inference>
To: Alice Chen <alice.chen@acme-analytics.com>
Cc: Lauren Bishop <lauren@redwood.inference>
Date: Wed, 2 Sep 2026 09:05:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Delta-tier overage & rebate framework

Hi Alice — thanks for the forecast, that helps. High level idea below and I attached a worked example (pricing_matrix_v3.xlsx) with sample numbers to make procurement comfortable.

Proposal (summary):
1) Commit tiers (monthly): Small / Mid / Large with stepped per-token baseline price. e.g. Tier A = $0.0009/token, Tier B = $0.0007/token, Tier C = $0.00055/token (numbers in attachment are illustrative).
2) Banded overage: on-demand pricing above commit is banded — first 10% over commit charged at a premium, 10-50% at a moderate overage rate, >50% at a negotiated surge rate. This prevents a single burst from being prohibitively expensive while still disincentivizing sustained over-commit.
3) Delta rebate (what you asked for): quarterly true-up where we compare rolling average committed spend vs actual on-demand. If a month is >commit but the trailing quarter average remains <= commit (i.e., spike is ephemeral), we apply a partial rebate on the excess tokens for that month (example: 40% rebate on the overage delta, applied at quarter end).
4) True-up cadence and caps: quarterly true-up with a per-quarter rebate cap (e.g., rebate cannot exceed 30% of commit in that quarter) and a one-time onboarding credit for the pilot month if you sign a 6-month pilot.

Next steps: I can convert the attached matrix to three numeric examples (pilot, launch month, steady-state) for the SOW. If procurement needs a guaranteed worst-case cap, we can add an explicit monthly overage ceiling at an agreed premium.

Thoughts? I can jump on a call with procurement + finance to walk through the sample math.

Best,
Ben Carter
Account Executive
Redwood Inference

From: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.com>

Subject: Procure/Billing follow-up from today's call

Jordan — thanks again for taking time today. Quick recap and the asks we surfaced on the call so your AP team can evaluate scope: 

- Procurement path: you mentioned centralized PO for larger vendors. Can you confirm whether ApexMed will raise a standard PO per billing period or per purchase order (monthly consolidated PO vs per-deployment PO)?
- Payment method: did you want to pay via PO (wire/ACH after invoice) or corporate card? Any corporate card limits we should know (single-transaction caps)?
- Marketplace interest: are you open to purchasing via your cloud marketplace (GCP Marketplace) if Redwood can be listed? That can simplify procurement for platform teams.
- Invoice requirements: AP asked for the invoice to include: PO number, billing address, tax ID/EIN, VAT breakdown (if applicable), and a single PDF per month with line-item usage. Can you confirm format (PDF is fine, or do you require XML/EDI)?
- Net terms: what is your target net terms (30/45/60)? Any internal approvals needed for 60+ day terms?
- Vendor setup: AP will need Redwood's W9 and vendor form. I attached a sample invoice and a vendor setup checklist for reference.

Attachments: redwood-invoice-sample.pdf (invoice sample, PDF), vendor-setup-checklist.xlsx (checklist, spreadsheet)

Next steps I propose:
1) You confirm PO cadence and preferred payment method.
2) I drop the W9 and our standard vendor contact info once you confirm you need it.
3) If marketplace is preferred, I loop Karthik for Marketplace onboarding details.

If helpful, we can do a short call with AP and our Billing Ops to walk through the sample invoice and vendor form. When works for you?

Thanks,
Ava Chen
Senior Sales Manager | Redwood Inference
ava.chen@redwood.com | (415) 555-0192

Redwood Inference

Hi Ava — thanks for the clear recap. From AP's side: 

- PO cadence: ApexMed will issue a monthly consolidated PO (one PO per month covering cloud vendor spend). That PO number must appear on any invoice we pay against.
- Payment method: PO + ACH (we don't put large vendor spend on a corporate card).
- Net terms: our standard is net 60 for enterprise vendors; exceptions require procurement lead approval. We'll request 60, but confirmers will be AP (Maya) and Procurement.
- Invoice format: PDF is fine. Our AP system can ingest a single PDF per month. We do not currently use XML/EDI for cloud vendor invoices.
- Billing address & Tax ID: we'll need the legal billing address and Redwood's EIN. Also will require a completed vendor form and W9.
- Marketplace: Procurement prefers direct vendor relationships for now; not opposed to marketplace in long term, but not a blocker for this procurement.

I'll loop in Maya for vendor form details. Please send the W9 and the vendor contact details.

Jordan Hughes

title: Re: DPA / TOMs — egress & retention workflow, subprocessors, DocuSign exec
preview:
From: Mike Jenkins <mike.jenkins@acme-corp.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
Cc: Acme Legal <legal@acme-corp.com>
Date: Wed, 02 Sep 2026 16:12:00 -0400
Subject: DPA: TOMs exhibit + subprocessors (initial list)

Ben —

Thanks for the update on the draft DPA. Our security and legal teams reviewed and have two immediate asks before we can proceed to signature:

1) Data egress & retention: we need a written workflow confirming how data is routed for inference, minimum retention times for request/response traces, and a clear mechanism to delete retained logs on customer request.
2) Subprocessor notification cadence: please confirm how Redwood will notify customers about new subprocessors (email window, automated portal, or per-contract notice).

Also attaching our standard DPA redline to help speed comments. Could you route to your security folks and provide a point-by-point response? We would prefer DocuSign for execution if the redlines are acceptable.

Attachments: Acme_DPA_Redlines_v1.docx (docx, 154KB)

Regards,
Mike Jenkins
Head of Cloud Procurement — Acme Corp



From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
To: Mike Jenkins <mike.jenkins@acme-corp.com>
Cc: Monica Patel <monica.patel@redwood.com>, Rishi Malhotra <rishi.malhotra@redwood.com>, Priyom Das <priyom.das@redwood.com>
Date: Thu, 03 Sep 2026 09:03:00 -0700
Subject: Re: DPA: TOMs exhibit + subprocessors (initial list)

Mike —

Thanks for the note and the redline. Looping in Monica (Legal) and Rishi (Security) — they will lead the point-by-point. Priyom from Product will confirm runtime routing details and retention behaviours.

High-level answers up front:
- Retention: by default we retain request/response traces and token-level logs for 30 days for debugging and eval monitoring; we can offer a 0-day retention option for enterprise customers (paired with Dedicated/Private deployments) and a contractual delete pathway for exported datasets.
- Egress & routing: production inference flows are routed to regionally pinned GPU pools; we support policy-based routing that can pin traffic to US-only hardware.
- Subprocessors: we notify customers via the console + email 30 days before onboarding a new subprocessor; we also publish a public subprocessor list updated weekly.

We expect to propose TOMs Exhibit revisions (encryption, key custody, and access matrix) by EOD. Will send a marked-up exhibit and a short point-by-point in a follow-up.

Best,
Ben
Director, Strategic Accounts | Redwood Inference
(415) 555-0199



From: Monica Patel <monica.patel@redwood.com>
To: Mike Jenkins <mike.jenkins@acme-corp.com>
Cc: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>, Rishi Malhotra <rishi.malhotra@redwood.com>, Priyom Das <priyom.das@redwood.com>
Date: Thu, 03 Sep 2026 11:21:00 -0700
Subject: Re: DPA: TOMs exhibit + subprocessors (detailed response + exhibit v2)

Mike —

Attached is our proposed TOMs Exhibit V2 (PDF) and an inline response to the redline you sent. Highlights:

1) Encryption-at-rest: We commit to AES-256 for all stored customer data. For hosted customers we manage KMS keys by default (Redwood-managed keys). For enterprise customers we offer BYOK (customer-managed keys in their KMS) and a hybrid envelope encryption option where customer key encrypts the data-encryption-key at rest.

2) Key rotation & emergency access: Keys rotate every 90 days for platform-managed keys; we will accept contractual requests for more frequent rotation windows. Emergency access (for lawful requests or incident response) requires a documented, auditable process and two-person approval with a recorded justification; we can supply audit logs for every key unwrap operation.

3) Access controls: Role-based access + Just-In-Time (JIT) admin escalation for ops during incident windows; tiered access for engineers vs. support vs. SRE.

4) Retention workflows: We propose a configuration-driven retention policy with three presets: 0d (no retention), 30d (default), 365d (audit/long-tail). For deletion requests we provide a 7-business-day SLA to remove request traces from hot storage and up to 30 days to purge from backups.

5) Subprocessor notifications: 30-day email notice plus a console banner for customers who opt-in to automatic alerts; we will maintain a webhook for MSPs/partners who want push updates.

Please see the appendix in the PDF for a sample operational runbook for egress requests and a proposed DocuSign execution block (two signatories: Redwood Legal + Acme Procurement).

Attachments: TOMs_Exhibit_Proposal_V2.pdf (pdf, 98KB)

Monica Patel
Senior Counsel, Privacy & Security | Redwood Inference



From: Mike Jenkins <mike.jenkins@acme-corp.com>
To: Monica Patel <monica.patel@redwood.com>
Cc: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>, Acme Legal <legal@acme-corp.com>
Date: Mon, 07 Sep 2026 10:05:00 -0400
Subject: Re: DPA: TOMs exhibit + subprocessors (follow-up comments)

Monica —

Thanks for the thorough response. Acme Legal had two narrow asks on the TOMs PDF:

A) Escrow / emergency key access: they request a commitment that emergency key escrow will not be used except under a court order OR a documented two-party exec approval with notification to Acme within 48 hours.
B) Retention deletion SLA: they want an explicit maximum purge window of 14 days (instead of Redwood's 30d backup purge) for PII from backups when the customer requests deletion; they understand some archival systems may have longer windows, but ask Redwood to commit to 14 calendar days for primary & backup purge for accounts flagged as "high-sensitivity".

They also asked whether Redwood would accept an e-signature flow using DocuSign with an embedded evidence bundle (TOMs PDF + audit table export) — and whether the DocuSign envelope can include a custodial chain-of-custody PDF generated after signature.

If you can confirm the escrow constraint and the 14-day purge for high-sensitivity accounts, we'll circulate for final internal approval and get DocuSign started.

Best,
Mike



From: Rishi Malhotra <rishi.malhotra@redwood.com>
To: Mike Jenkins <mike.jenkins@acme-corp.com>, Monica Patel <monica.patel@redwood.com>, Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
Date: Tue, 08 Sep 2026 07:46:00 -0700
Subject: Re: DPA: TOMs exhibit + subprocessors (technical confirmation)

Mike / Monica —

Technical confirmation and proposed small edits below. Quoted for clarity:

> Emergency key escrow will not be used except under a court order OR a documented two-party exec approval with notification to Acme within 48 hours.

We can accept that language with one addition: any two-party exec approval must be captured in an auditable ticketing workflow (we propose using our incident tracker) and be constrained to a specific key-id and time-window (e.g., 24 hours). We'll include the ticket-id in the post-event audit record delivered to the customer.

> 14-day purge for high-sensitivity accounts

We can commit to 14 calendar days for removal from hot storage and primary backups for accounts designated as "high-sensitivity" by contract. For deep cold archives (offsite immutable backups) we will make best-effort deletions and will provide a documented remediation plan; any immutable-retention requirements must be flagged at onboarding.

Regarding DocuSign: yes — we support embedding an evidence bundle. After execution we can auto-generate a custody report (PDF) that includes envelope id, signer certs, and a hash of the final TOMs PDF. We will attach that PDF to the signed envelope and provide a download link to both parties.

Suggested redline: add a small clause to Section 3.2 (Key Escrow) requiring ticketed justification and the ticket id included in legal notice to the customer.

If that works, Monica — please confirm final wording and I can update the TOMs PDF and return a signed DocuSign envelope for Redwood. Ben — happy to run the DocuSign flow when legal signs off.

Thanks,
Rishi
Principal Security Engineer | Redwood Inference



From: Monica Patel <monica.patel@redwood.com>
To: Mike Jenkins <mike.jenkins@acme-corp.com>, Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>, Rishi Malhotra <rishi.malhotra@redwood.com>, Priyom Das <priyom.das@redwood.com>
Date: Fri, 10 Sep 2026 13:48:00 -0700
Subject: Re: DPA: TOMs exhibit + subprocessors (final & DocuSign next steps)

Mike —

We agree to the two items as edited by Rishi. Final confirmatory points:

• Emergency key escrow: Redwood will not use escrowed keys except under a court order OR a documented two-person executive approval captured as a ticket in our incident system. The ticket id and justification will be included in the post-event notification to the customer within 48 hours.
• High-sensitivity purge SLA: Redwood will remove PII from hot storage and primary backups within 14 calendar days for accounts flagged as high-sensitivity in the contract. Deep-archive removal remains best-effort and is excluded from the 14-day guarantee unless specified at onboarding.

We have updated the TOMs Exhibit PDF and added the small redline to Section 3.2. Ben — please initiate the DocuSign envelope as discussed (two signers: Redwood Legal / Acme Procurement). I've attached the final PDF and prepared the evidence bundle instructions for the envelope.

Attachments: TOMs_Exhibit_Proposal_V2.pdf (updated) (pdf, 104KB), DocuSign_Envelope_Acme_20260910.pdf (pdf stub, 12KB)

Monica Patel
Senior Counsel, Privacy & Security | Redwood Inference

>
> Thanks for the quick turn. Acme Legal approves the edits; please proceed with DocuSign. We'll expect the custody PDF attached post-signature.

> Mike

To: Mike Jenkins <mike.jenkins@acme-corp.com>, Monica Patel <monica.patel@redwood.com>, Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
Date: Tue, 08 Sep 2026 07:46:00 -0700
Subject: Re: DPA: TOMs exhibit + subprocessors (technical confirmation)

Mike / Monica —

Technical confirmation and proposed small edits below. Quoted for clarity:

> Emergency key escrow will not be used except under a court order OR a documented two-party exec approval with notification to Acme within 48 hours.

We can accept that language with one addition: any two-party exec approval must be captured in an auditable ticketing workflow (we propose using our incident tracker) and be constrained to a specific key-id and time-window (e.g., 24 hours). We'll include the ticket-id in the post-event audit record delivered to the customer.

> 14-day purge for high-sensitivity accounts

We can commit to 14 calendar days for removal from hot storage and primary backups for accounts designated as "high-sensitivity" by contract. For deep cold archives (offsite immutable backups) we will make best-effort deletions and will provide a documented remediation plan; any immutable-retention requirements must be flagged at onboarding.

Regarding DocuSign: yes — we support embedding an evidence bundle. After execution we can auto-generate a custody report (PDF) that includes envelope id, signer certs, and a hash of the final TOMs PDF. We will attach that PDF to the signed envelope and provide a download link to both parties.

Suggested redline: add a small clause to Section 3.2 (Key Escrow) requiring ticketed justification and the ticket id included in legal notice to the customer.

If that works, Monica — please confirm final wording and I can update the TOMs PDF and return a signed DocuSign envelope for Redwood. Ben — happy to run the DocuSign flow when legal signs off.

Thanks,
Rishi
Principal Security Engineer | Redwood Inference

Date: Fri, 10 Sep 2026 13:48:00 -0700
Subject: Re: DPA: TOMs exhibit + subprocessors (final & DocuSign next steps)

Mike —

We agree to the two items as edited by Rishi. Final confirmatory points:

• Emergency key escrow: Redwood will not use escrowed keys except under a court order OR a documented two-person executive approval captured as a ticket in our incident system. The ticket id and justification will be included in the post-event notification to the customer within 48 hours.
• High-sensitivity purge SLA: Redwood will remove PII from hot storage and primary backups within 14 calendar days for accounts flagged as high-sensitivity in the contract. Deep-archive removal remains best-effort and is excluded from the 14-day guarantee unless specified at onboarding.

We have updated the TOMs Exhibit PDF and added the small redline to Section 3.2. Ben — please initiate the DocuSign envelope as discussed (two signers: Redwood Legal / Acme Procurement). I've attached the final PDF and prepared the evidence bundle instructions for the envelope.

title: Billing handoff: invoice origin & temporary portal bridge
preview:
From: Grace Foster <grace.foster@saffronbio.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
Date: Tue, 7 Sep 2026 09:14:00 -0700
Subject: Billing handoff: invoice origin & temporary portal bridge

Hi Ben,

We're getting close to go-live for the Redwood integration. AP has a few last-mile questions before we route invoices into our system. Quick summary:

- PO number for the subscription is attached (PO 98765).
- We prefer receiving invoices via your portal (for matching), but want PDF/email copies until our SFTP endpoint is ready.
- We need an XML invoice sample that maps to our AP ingestion fields (VAT breakdown must be explicit).

Can you confirm:
1) Who will be the invoice \"originator\" in your system (billing@redwood.com or the portal service)?
2) Can Redwood send a daily email copy to APteam@saffronbio.com until Oct 1, 2026?
3) Do you have an example XML that includes VAT tags for our mapping team?

Appreciate a short checklist so I can share with our AP lead.

Thanks,
Grace Foster
AP Manager
Saffron Biotech

Attachments: Saffron_PO_98765.pdf, Saffron_VAT_certificate.pdf

From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
To: Grace Foster <grace.foster@saffronbio.com>
Cc: Kimberly Park <kimberly.park@redwood.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>
Date: Tue, 7 Sep 2026 11:03:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Billing handoff: invoice origin & temporary portal bridge

Grace — thanks for the clear summary and the PO. Looping in Kimberly from Billing Ops and Marissa (AE) to coordinate the onboarding steps. Quick answers up front:

- Invoice originator: by default invoices are issued by our billing system (billing@redwood.com) and visible in the portal. We can set the portal as the canonical source and still send email copies.
- Temporary email copies: yes — we can add APteam@saffronbio.com to the daily invoice distribution queue through the cutover date.
- XML schema: attaching our invoice XML schema and a small sample. Kimberly: can you confirm the VAT field mapping (gross/net/VAT lines) matches Saffron's requirement?

Proposed next steps (short):
1) Redwood to provide XML sample + mapping notes (today).
2) Redwood to enable daily email copies to APteam@saffronbio.com (cutover: immediate until Oct 1).
3) Coordinate test file to Saffron's SFTP when ready (target before Sep 24).

If that works I'll send a one-page mutual action plan and a DocuSign link for the billing addendum (small change to delivery terms).

Ben Carter
Senior Sales Engineer
Redwood Inference

Attachments: Redwood_Invoice_XML_Schema_v2.xml

From: Kimberly Park <kimberly.park@redwood.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>, Grace Foster <grace.foster@saffronbio.com>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>
Date: Wed, 8 Sep 2026 08:20:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Billing handoff: invoice origin & temporary portal bridge

Thanks Ben — confirming from Billing Ops side. A few operational notes:

- Portal-first / email-copy hybrid: we will mark the portal as primary source for reconciliation (so invoice IDs and timestamps will appear there). Email copies will be identical PDFs generated at emission time; they include the same invoice ID for matching.
- XML fields: our schema includes these VAT-related tags: <TotalNet>, <VATRate>, <VATAmount>, <GrossTotal>. If Saffron needs an additional breakdown (e.g., VAT by jurisdiction), we can include an optional <VATBreakdown> section.
- PO handling: we will place PO 98765 in the invoice header and populate Saffron's remittance details.
- Temporary delivery: we'll set daily distribution at 03:00 UTC. Please confirm the correct AP contact and any IP allowlist if you need to receive via API/SFTP.

Grace — attaching onboarding checklist and portal_onboarding_steps.pdf to make the setup smoother. Let me know if you need a signed change notice — Ben will send the DocuSign for the delivery addendum if required.

Thanks,
Kimberly Park
Billing Operations Manager
Redwood Inference

Attachments: portal_onboarding_steps.pdf

From: Grace Foster <grace.foster@saffronbio.com>
To: Kimberly Park <kimberly.park@redwood.com>, Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
Date: Thu, 9 Sep 2026 10:45:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Billing handoff: invoice origin & temporary portal bridge

Kimberly — this looks good. Couple clarifications from AP:

- Confirmed: APteam@saffronbio.com is the email to receive daily invoice copies. Please include \"Saffron PO 98765\" in the subject line for quick filters.
- XML mapping: we need the optional <VATBreakdown> — we have EU/UK and a few domestic invoices so jurisdiction tags are required.
- IP allowlist: we'll provide our SFTP/IP details (sending separately to secure@redwood.com).

We won't need a signed change notice for the temporary email copies, but we do require the billing delivery addendum to show on file (non-financial). If you can send the DocuSign link, I'll route to procurement.

Thanks — attaching the PO and VAT cert again in case the first attachment didn't come through.

Best,
Grace

Perfect — thanks Grace. Quick summary and action owner list to close this out:

- Kimberly: add <VATBreakdown> to XML schema and send a sample mapping file (action: Sep 9).

Date: Tue, 7 Sep 2026 15:12:00 +0000
Subject: Draft MAP — SDK/Streaming Integration: owners, blockers, cutover target

Alex — thanks for a productive discovery this morning. Per our call I drafted a short Mutual Action Plan (MAP) slice focused on the integration build milestones you asked about (SDK integration, streaming, tool calling, structured outputs). Please review and flag any gaps. I attached a working matrix (sdk_integration_matrix.xlsx) and the MAP PDF.

Summary (proposed milestones, owners, targets):
1) Contract & SOW signed — Owner: Nimble Procurement / Redwood Sales (Marissa) — Target: 2026-09-21 — Dependency: countersigned SOW + pricing confirmation.
2) Network & credentials (VPC peering, firewall rules, API keys) — Owner: Nimble IT (Jenna) / Redwood Infra (Soojin) — Target: 2026-09-14 — Dependency: network team availability + IP allowlist.
3) SDK integration (JavaScript + Python) — Owner: Nimble Eng (Jenna) / Redwood SE (Dev Patel) — Target: 2026-09-24 — Deliverables: sample client, auth flow, streaming client example.
4) Tool calling & auth scopes (tool endpoints + OAuth scopes) — Owner: Nimble Eng / Redwood Eng — Target: 2026-09-24 — Dependency: API contract + test tool endpoints.
5) Structured outputs & schema validation (JSON schema + examples) — Owner: Nimble Product / Redwood Solutions — Target: 2026-09-28 — Deliverables: schema doc + test prompts.
6) Staging validation (end-to-end tests, perf smoke, token/cost estimate) — Owner: Redwood CS (Karthik) / Nimble QA — Target: 2026-09-30.
7) Cutover / enablement (rollout + enablement session for product teams) — Owner: Marissa / Nimble PM — Target: 2026-10-05.

Open blockers we discussed: SOC2 evidence for security team (you mentioned this — attach request), peering windows for your infra team, and SSO mapping for tool calls.

Asks for this thread: please reply with any ownership changes, feasibility of these dates, and who on your side will be primary for network and SDK work. If we align, I’ll update the MAP PDF and push a canary timeline into the shared Drive + HubSpot.

title: Pilot success criteria, contractual sentinels & test harness
preview:
From: Mina Haddad <mina@redwood.com>
To: Claire Hughes <claire@healthsync.com>
Cc: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa@redwood.com>
Date: 2026-09-08T09:12:00-07:00
Subject: Pilot success criteria, contractual sentinels & test harness

Claire —

Thanks again for taking the call today. As discussed, attached is a draft mutual action plan (MAP_v1.xlsx) and an initial acceptance-test matrix. I wanted to capture the success criteria and the "contractual sentinels" we talked about so there is no ambiguity at handoff. High-level asks below: 

- Acceptance tests (what passes the pilot):
  - Accuracy / Concordance: >= 92% on the 2,000-case sample set (ground-truth annotated by HealthSync SMEs)
  - Latency: P95 generation time <= 220ms for on-prem inference (dataset distribution attached)
  - Cost/throughput: Sustained 50 qps with 5% tail above baseline when batched as recommended
  - Safety/compliance: All outputs must pass the redaction rule set and produce no PHI leaks in the sample traces

- Exit gates (automatic fail/rollback triggers):
  - Any single-day regression >6 percentage points in agreement rate vs baseline
  - More than two production incidents in a 7-day window related to model hallucination or PHI exposure
  - Failure to provide SOC2 evidence requested by payer contracts within the 10-business-day window

- Contractual sentinels (contract-language asks to include with pilot SOW):
  - 30-day remediation period for quality regressions before escalation to credits
  - Canary rollback policy: automatic fallback to the latest verified checkpoint when P95 latency exceeds target for 3 consecutive hours
  - Data-retention & deletion clause aligned to HealthSync's policy (30-day retention in preview environment)

Next steps I propose:
1) You and Jordan confirm the listed thresholds or suggest alternate numbers by EOD Thursday.
2) HealthSync sends back the annotated 2k sample and any redaction rules (we can sign the mutual NDA addendum if needed).
3) We lock the MAP and convert to a DocuSign SOW (Ben will run the contract language for sentinels).

Attachment: MAP_v1.xlsx (outline of milestones, success flags, and owners)

Cheers,

Mina Haddad
Senior Sales Engineer | Redwood Inference

Redwood Inference

title: Sparrow Health — onramp connector recommendation and action plan
preview:
From: Chloe Martin <chloe.martin@redwood.ai>
To: Tom Blake <tom.blake@sparrowhealth.com>
Cc: Hannah Schmitt <hannah.schmitt@redwood.ai>, Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>, Kimberly Park <kimberly.park@redwood.ai>
Date: Thu, 3 Sep 2026 15:22:00 +0000
Subject: Sparrow Health — onramp connector recommendation and action plan

Hi Tom / Elena,

Thanks again for the run-through earlier today. Based on the topology you shared and Sparrow's security constraints (no inbound public endpoints allowed, strict egress filtering, required audit logs), we've sketched three feasible ingress options and a recommended approach. Summary first, details below.

Recommendation (short):
- Primary: PrivateLink (interface endpoints) into a dedicated, customer-isolated VPC endpoint service hosted by Redwood for Sparrow's production tenant.
- Fallback / pilot: Outbound site-to-site (S2S) VPN initiated from Sparrow to a Redwood gateway for initial integration and smoke tests.

Why this combo?
- PrivateLink gives the strongest isolation and simplest firewall posture for your security team (no public DNS records, VPC endpoint policies, per-ENI flow logs).
- However, given Sparrow's existing on-prem appliance constraints and timeline, an outbound S2S VPN for the pilot avoids firewall reconfiguration and lets us validate traffic patterns quickly.

Brief pros/cons we considered: 
- VPC Peering: low-latency, low-cost, but requires peering across accounts/regions and does not work across AWS accounts where we need strict tenant isolation. Peering also exposes route tables and can complicate cross-account audit boundaries.
- PrivateLink: best for isolation, per-endpoint IAM/policy control, and works without exposing private CIDR ranges to us. Slightly higher per-GB costs and additional ENI management, but aligns with compliance goals.
- S2S VPN: fastest to get running for your ops team (outbound tunnels only) and minimal changes on your side, but longer-term we recommend migrating to PrivateLink for stronger auditability and reduced operational blast radius.

Operational asks from Sparrow for PrivateLink path:
1) Create a VPC in your AWS account with at least one private subnet and allow interface endpoint ENIs to be created (we will share exact subnet/CIDR guidance).
2) Confirm egress rules that allow outbound connectivity to Redwood gateway IPs (we've included a short checklist in operational_checklist_v1.md attached).
3) Approve a limited set of roles to accept the endpoint service (we provide IAM policy snippets).

Pilot timeline proposal:
- Day 0-3: Stand up S2S VPN tunnel (outbound from Sparrow) + smoke tests. Owner: Sparrow NetOps (Tom).
- Day 4-10: Configure PrivateLink endpoint; Redwood will provision the service and run integration tests. Owner: Redwood (Chloe + Hannah).
- Day 11-15: Cutover traffic to PrivateLink; retire VPN or keep as warm fallback.

Attachments: sparrow_network_diagram_v1.pdf, operational_checklist_v1.md (attached).

Can you confirm whether Sparrow can create the short-lived test VPC and whether your firewall team can allow the outbound tunnel to 34.198.22.45/32 (Redwood test gateway) for the pilot? If yes, we'll schedule the pilot kickoff for next week.

Thanks,

Chloe Martin
Senior Solutions Architect
Redwood Inference
chloe.martin@redwood.ai
+1 (415) 555-0193

---
(attachment: sparrow_network_diagram_v1.pdf — network topology sketch)

Chloe,

Appreciate the write-up and the clarity. A couple of quick clarifications from our side: 

- Our security team is strongly biased toward not exposing any inbound connections. They prefer everything to be outbound from our edge.
- We can spin up a temporary VPC in our account for the interface endpoint but it will require a change request (2–3 business days).
- For the VPN option, we will need the exact PSK options and if you support IKEv2 with strongSwan on our Juniper boxes.

Questions: 
1) If we do the S2S tunnel first, will that give you the same logs/observability you need for early QA, or do you need PrivateLink to be in place to validate end-to-end behavior?
2) Any material SLA difference between traffic over PrivateLink vs VPN we should be aware of?

If pilot can start next Wednesday, we can have NetOps dedicate some time. I don't think we can approve inbound endpoint ENIs without sign-off from Cloud Security, but we can do the outbound tunnel in the meantime.

From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>
To: Chloe Martin <chloe.martin@redwood.ai>, Hannah Schmitt <hannah.schmitt@redwood.ai>
Cc: Kimberly Park <kimberly.park@redwood.ai>, Tom Blake <tom.blake@sparrowhealth.com>
Date: Mon, 7 Sep 2026 14:02:00 +0000
Subject: Re: Sparrow Health — onramp connector recommendation and action plan

Thanks — wanted to drop the rough cost/ops notes so the deal team has numbers for the procurement thread. Quick bullets: 

- PrivateLink path: per-ENI hourly + cross-AZ data processing. Estimate for Sparrow (steady 500 GB/day): ~USD 6k–8k/month (network egress + endpoint hourly amortized). Exact depends on cross-AZ patterns; see attached cost_estimate_onramp.csv.
- VPN-only pilot: near-zero Redwood infra delta but higher ops time on your side for tunnel maintenance; should be cost-effective short-term (~USD 500–800/mo for gateway egress).
- Peering: not recommended here because we need tenant isolation for regulatory reasons; peering also blocks our ability to offer per-tenant auditing.

Operational notes: 
- We'll need a support ticket with our infra team to provision an endpoint service (internal lead: Hannah).
- For PrivateLink we will request that Sparrow allocate two /27 subnets (for redundancy) in different AZs — we'll provide exact CIDR guidance.
- If Sparrow wants to keep the VPN as a warm fallback, we recommend monitoring and an automated failover policy in the Redwood console (we can provide a runbook).

Ben Carter
