text:
From: Chloe Martin <chloe.martin@redwood.ai>
To: Tom Blake <tom.blake@sparrowhealth.com>
Cc: Hannah Schmitt <hannah.schmitt@redwood.ai>, Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>, Kimberly Park <kimberly.park@redwood.ai>
Date: Thu, 3 Sep 2026 15:22:00 +0000
Subject: Sparrow Health — onramp connector recommendation and action plan

Hi Tom / Elena,

Thanks again for the run-through earlier today. Based on the topology you shared and Sparrow's security constraints (no inbound public endpoints allowed, strict egress filtering, required audit logs), we've sketched three feasible ingress options and a recommended approach. Summary first, details below.

Recommendation (short):
- Primary: PrivateLink (interface endpoints) into a dedicated, customer-isolated VPC endpoint service hosted by Redwood for Sparrow's production tenant.
- Fallback / pilot: Outbound site-to-site (S2S) VPN initiated from Sparrow to a Redwood gateway for initial integration and smoke tests.

Why this combo?
- PrivateLink gives the strongest isolation and simplest firewall posture for your security team (no public DNS records, VPC endpoint policies, per-ENI flow logs).
- However, given Sparrow's existing on-prem appliance constraints and timeline, an outbound S2S VPN for the pilot avoids firewall reconfiguration and lets us validate traffic patterns quickly.

Brief pros/cons we considered: 
- VPC Peering: low-latency, low-cost, but requires peering across accounts/regions and does not work across AWS accounts where we need strict tenant isolation. Peering also exposes route tables and can complicate cross-account audit boundaries.
- PrivateLink: best for isolation, per-endpoint IAM/policy control, and works without exposing private CIDR ranges to us. Slightly higher per-GB costs and additional ENI management, but aligns with compliance goals.
- S2S VPN: fastest to get running for your ops team (outbound tunnels only) and minimal changes on your side, but longer-term we recommend migrating to PrivateLink for stronger auditability and reduced operational blast radius.

Operational asks from Sparrow for PrivateLink path:
1) Create a VPC in your AWS account with at least one private subnet and allow interface endpoint ENIs to be created (we will share exact subnet/CIDR guidance).
2) Confirm egress rules that allow outbound connectivity to Redwood gateway IPs (we've included a short checklist in operational_checklist_v1.md attached).
3) Approve a limited set of roles to accept the endpoint service (we provide IAM policy snippets).

Pilot timeline proposal:
- Day 0-3: Stand up S2S VPN tunnel (outbound from Sparrow) + smoke tests. Owner: Sparrow NetOps (Tom).
- Day 4-10: Configure PrivateLink endpoint; Redwood will provision the service and run integration tests. Owner: Redwood (Chloe + Hannah).
- Day 11-15: Cutover traffic to PrivateLink; retire VPN or keep as warm fallback.

Attachments: sparrow_network_diagram_v1.pdf, operational_checklist_v1.md (attached).

Can you confirm whether Sparrow can create the short-lived test VPC and whether your firewall team can allow the outbound tunnel to 34.198.22.45/32 (Redwood test gateway) for the pilot? If yes, we'll schedule the pilot kickoff for next week.

Thanks,

Chloe Martin
Senior Solutions Architect
Redwood Inference
chloe.martin@redwood.ai
+1 (415) 555-0193

---
(attachment: sparrow_network_diagram_v1.pdf — network topology sketch)

Thanks — wanted to drop the rough cost/ops notes so the deal team has numbers for the procurement thread. Quick bullets: 

- PrivateLink path: per-ENI hourly + cross-AZ data processing. Estimate for Sparrow (steady 500 GB/day): ~USD 6k–8k/month (network egress + endpoint hourly amortized). Exact depends on cross-AZ patterns; see attached cost_estimate_onramp.csv.
- VPN-only pilot: near-zero Redwood infra delta but higher ops time on your side for tunnel maintenance; should be cost-effective short-term (~USD 500–800/mo for gateway egress).
- Peering: not recommended here because we need tenant isolation for regulatory reasons; peering also blocks our ability to offer per-tenant auditing.

Operational notes:

- For PrivateLink we will request that Sparrow allocate two /27 subnets (for redundancy) in different AZs — we'll provide exact CIDR guidance.
- If Sparrow wants to keep the VPN as a warm fallback, we recommend monitoring and an automated failover policy in the Redwood console (we can provide a runbook).

Samir — thanks for the quick reply. I can take the streaming/reconnect portion. A few quick notes so we set expectations:
- Streaming is at-least-once delivery by default for the public hosted path; we have mechanisms for prefix caching and token-level resume keys.
- For client reconnects we can walk through both the client-side SDK approach (resume tokens) and the server-side fallback we use when KV cache misses occur.
- We'll include a short snippet showing function/tool call orchestration (sync vs async) and an example of how callbacks are routed back to a small webhook consumer.

I'll attach an early draft of the runtime diagram (runtime-architecture-draft.vsdx) so your infra team can annotate before the session. Small ask: confirm whether you prefer the meeting in PT or ET timezone for your wider team — we can accommodate both.

Rishi

From: Irene Choi <irene.choi@redwoodinference.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwoodinference.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwoodinference.com>

Date: 2026-09-03T16:24:00-07:00
Subject: Re: Private Deployment: VPC handoff & on-prem packaging — next steps

All —

I ran a quick validation against our appliance and the install flow. Summary of port/egress needs (for TGW model):
- Ingress (from Lavid VPC to appliance VPC): TCP 443 (mTLS API endpoint), TCP 8443 (admin UI), UDP 123 (NTP)
- Outbound (from appliance to Redwood hosted services): TCP 443 to list of Redwood egress IPs (we'll need Samir's confirmed list to finalize ACLs)
- KV/cache sync: TCP 7000–7010 (ephemeral between appliance nodes) — these are internal only and can be over the peered path; no public exposure.

Certs: appliance supports PEM and PKCS12; mTLS with customer CA is supported and we can load CA bundles at install time. Installer script expects: 
- server_cert.pem, server_key.pem (PKCS8 ok), ca_bundle.pem
- optional: pkcs12.pfx if you prefer a single file (installer will extract).

I will drop a minimal onprem-package checklist (text) into the thread and attach the port matrix. Deliverable attached: onprem-package-checklist.txt

Next: Samir, can you confirm the Redwood egress IP allowlist and provide the CA bundle you want us to install (or confirm you'll hand off at install time)?

Thanks,
Irene

irene.choi@redwoodinference.com

title: Reservation off-ramps & sizing: MAP for Quicksilver Health
preview:
From: Leah Summers <leah@quicksilverhealth.com>
To: Olga Petrov <olga.petrov@redwood.com>
Cc: Amit Shah <amit.shah@quicksilverhealth.com>
Date: 2026-09-05T09:12:00-07:00
Subject: MAP kickoff: lead times + sizing inputs

Hi Olga —

Thanks for the intro last week. We want to get a short MAP in place that ties a three-stage reservation (pilot, tranche 1, tranche 2) to clear evaluation gates and off-ramps. Target close is end of October but procurement needs firm lead times and a fallback plan if H100 availability shifts.

Quick context: Quicksilver is building a clinical summarization feature that will need a steady 80–120 rps inference path during peak hours and sub-50ms p50 for short prompts. We expect a 30% burst factor for batch QA runs.

As next steps can you share:
- typical Dedicated private lead times (order -> provision) for H100-equivalent pools
- a sizing sketch for an initial 4-week pilot vs. production (GPU counts / memory profile)
- suggested off-ramps if vendor lead times slip (e.g., fallback quantized variant / regional routing)

Attaching our internal evaluation checklist (qsh_map_v3.pdf). Procurement will be on a separate thread but cc'd here.

Leah
Product Lead, Quicksilver Health

-- attachment: qsh_map_v3.pdf (pdf)


From: Olga Petrov <olga.petrov@redwood.com>
To: Leah Summers <leah@quicksilverhealth.com>
Cc: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>, Priyom Das <priyom.das@redwood.com>
Date: 2026-09-05T11:03:00-07:00
Subject: Re: MAP kickoff: lead times + sizing inputs

Hi Leah —

Thanks for the clear ask and for attaching the checklist. Looping in Ben (capacity planning) and Priyom (onboarding playbook) to own the sizing and the MAP milestones.

Top-level answers up front:
- Typical Dedicated private lead time (H100-equivalent): 8–12 weeks from signed SOW to warm pool (depends on region and rack availability). For Reserved Dedicated in our hosted clouds we can do a faster 4–6 week path if you accept mixed-node placement (H100 + H100-lite equivalents).
- Pilot sizing (4 weeks): we recommend a mixed 6-GPU pool (A100/H100-lite) with prefix caching and conservative batching to validate 50ms p50 for the short prompt profile.
- Production tranche 1: 24–32 GPUs (H100-equivalent) to support sustained 80–120 rps with headroom; tranche 2 scales to 64+ depending on model and quantization.

Off-ramps we routinely use:
1) Regional routing to lower-lead-time zones (shorter warm-up)
2) Quantized model variant (8-bit) as a temporary cost/availability hedge
3) Autoscaling to burst into hosted reserved capacity with a slightly higher cost basis

Leah / Amit —

Appreciate the timeline. Below is a concise mutual action plan (MAP) draft with firm gating and off-ramps. If this looks right we will convert to a single-page PDF for signatures and attach to the SOW.

MAP: Quicksilver Health — Clinical Summarization
- Objective: Deliver production-ready inference for summarization with p50 <= 50ms for short prompts and sustained 80–120 rps.

Key dates (assumes signed SOW by 2026-09-20):
- Pilot provision (accelerated hosted reserved): 2026-10-04 (4 weeks)
- Warm pool dedicated (private/VPC) earliest commit: 2026-11-15 (8–12 weeks; we will confirm by 2026-09-22 after capacity check)
- Tranche 1 scale target: 2026-12-06 (post-acceptance + 2 weeks)

Gating (all must clear to move to production tranche):
1) Pilot acceptance: latency and throughput tests (bench scripts, 1-week smoke + 1-week soak)
2) Security: SOC2 scoping and attestation for required workloads (Priyom to provide required checklist; target complete by 2026-10-20)
3) Regression: prompt-set based quality tests (failures trigger rollback to quantized fallback)

Off-ramps & operational triggers:
- If Dedicated warm pool slips >14 days from committed date: Redwood will activate hosted reserved burst capacity in-region (same-day activation for small burst, 48 hours for larger) and offer a one-time credit equal to 10% of the first-month committed fee.
- If model availability causes >25% performance regression: automatic fallback to quantized variant with notification and post-mortem within 72 hours.
- If regional routing used as fallback: we will warm 50% of KV cache ahead of cutover and run a traffic shadow for 24 hours before full switch.

- Ben: finalize gpu_sizing_estimate.xlsx and flag the exact H100-lite equivalents we plan to provision by 2026-09-16.

Hi Ben / Marissa,

Thanks again for the walkthrough yesterday — the Helix team is keen to move forward with the enterprise pilot next month. A few billing asks from procurement so we can issue a PO: 

- Can Redwood accept Net60 for this pilot? Our finance team prefers Net60 on pilot contracts to align with monthly close.

Date: Tue, 1 Sep 2026 11:03:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Request: Net60 for pilot + VAT evidence and payment cadence

Claire — thanks for the clear ask and the SOW. Really excited to get Helix onboard. A couple of notes from our side: 

Short answer: we can be flexible but need to balance credit exposure for pilot customers. Proposed options we can implement quickly: 

1) Preferred compromise (fastest): 30% onboarding deposit + Net30 on subsequent monthly invoices. This gets you operational quickly and reduces our short-term credit risk.

2) Alternate (finance-approved but requires review): Net45 / Net60 on usage invoices with a short-term credit line capped at $125k for the first 60 days while we collect the PO and remit the deposit. We can revisit limits at day 60 once usage patterns are established.

3) If Helix needs Net60 outright with a $200k credit limit, we will require a completed credit application and a 60–90 day finance review before enabling that level.

Action items I recommend now: 
- Helix confirms in writing which option you prefer (1/2/3).
- If option 2 or 3, please complete the attached fillable credit app and provide a VAT certificate for the billing entity.
- If you can sign SOW and issue PO with a 30% deposit (option 1), we can start onboarding immediately and invoice for the deposit on receipt of PO.

I've looped in Monica from Finance to run the credit check and finalize the short-term limit. Marissa will own contract signature questions.

Attaching: SOW (clean copy) and Credit App (fillable).

Best,
Ben Carter
Enterprise Sales | Redwood Inference
ben.carter@redwood.com | +1 (415) 555-0198

After 60 days we will re-evaluate limit based on usage and payment timeliness.
- For Net60 requests (Option 3), require completed credit application + two supplier references and allow 7–10 business days for underwriting; consider partial card-on-file guarantees if Helix prefers no deposit.

- VAT certificate for Helix EU billing entity (PDF)

- Go-live target: 2026-11-15 (soft launch with 10% traffic)

title: Network handoff and environment validation — Nimbus Health private deployment
preview:
From: Sarah Turner <sarah.turner@nimbushealth.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood-inference.com>
Cc: Infra Admin <infra-admin@nimbushealth.com>
Date: Fri, 05 Sep 2026 09:12:00 -0700
Subject: Private deployment — initial network info and questionnaire

Hi Ava,

Thanks again for walking us through the private deployment options yesterday. Per our call, attached is the initial environment questionnaire (filled out partially) and a network inventory export from our infra team. We wanted to surface a few open items before the formal onboarding kickoff: 

- Whether Redwood supports AWS Transit Gateway peering or if we should expect VPC Peering only.
- Preferred approach for egress filtering: proxy in the VPC vs. NAT + firewall rules.
- Requirements for certificate provisioning if we use PrivateLink (do you require customer-managed certs?).
- Any minimum MTU/PKT settings you recommend for the KV cache traffic.

We also included a diagram of our current network zones and the CIDR blocks we plan to reserve. Please let us know if anything looks off — we'd like to get the environment validation checklist so we can run a wiring window next week (Mon/Tue preferred).

Attachments: nimbus_private_onboarding_questionnaire.pdf, infra-network-inventory.csv

Thanks,
Sarah Turner
Head of Cloud Ops
Nimbus Health



From: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood-inference.com>
To: Sarah Turner <sarah.turner@nimbushealth.com>
Cc: David Kim <david.kim@redwood-inference.com>, Lauren Bishop <lauren.bishop@redwood-inference.com>
Date: Fri, 05 Sep 2026 10:08:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Private deployment — initial network info and questionnaire

Hi Sarah —

Appreciate the questionnaire and the network export. I reviewed the attachment quickly and flagged a few items inline. High level answers to your bullets:

- Transit Gateway: Redwood currently supports both Transit Gateway and VPC peering for hosted-private setups. Transit Gateway is preferred when you have multiple VPCs to route through (it simplifies routing), but we can work with single VPC peering if you need a simpler model. We'll want the TGW attachment ID if you go that route.

- Egress filtering: Our recommended pattern for low-latency inference is to keep egress NAT at the VPC edge and use security groups + NACLs for pod-level restricts. If you need deep outbound filtering (URL-level), place a proxy in the private subnet — Redwood's runtime will work fine behind a proxy but we will need outbound connectivity to our metrics and health endpoints (list below).

- Certs for PrivateLink: We accept Redwood-managed certs for the PrivateLink endpoint, but for strict compliance customers you can present a customer-managed cert via ACM. We will document the CN requirements in the wiring guide.

- MTU: No special MTU required for KV traffic; standard 1500 works in most AWS setups. If you have jumbo frames on-prem, let us know and we can validate.

Next steps: I attached our concise "VPC wiring guide" and a checklist that we use during the environment validation run. Can you confirm a 2-hour window on Monday 09/07 09:00-11:00 PT for a wiring test? David will lead the packet-level validation and Lauren will run the rollout checklist.

Outbound endpoints (for firewall/proxy):
- metrics.redwood-inference.com:443
- health.redwood-inference.com:443
- s3-us-east-1.amazonaws.com (for model artifact pulls)

Attachments: redwood-vpc-wiring-guide.pdf

Best,
Ava Chen
Senior Solutions Engineer | Redwood Inference
ava.chen@redwood-inference.com



From: David Kim <david.kim@redwood-inference.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood-inference.com>, Sarah Turner <sarah.turner@nimbushealth.com>
Cc: Lauren Bishop <lauren.bishop@redwood-inference.com>, Stephanie Nguyen <stephanie.nguyen@redwood-inference.com>
Date: Fri, 05 Sep 2026 11:22:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Private deployment — initial network info and questionnaire

Sarah —

Quick follow-up with specifics so your infra team can prep: 

1) Ports/protocols we will exercise during wired validation (both directions where noted):
   - TCP 443 (TLS) outbound to metrics/health endpoints
   - TCP 22 (SSH) inbound to jump host during the wiring window (we'll use ephemeral keys)
   - TCP 2379 (etcd) internal only — no external exposure
   - UDP 123 (NTP) outbound to your NTP pool or upstream (if you block NTP, let us know; we can fallback to system clock sync)

2) CIDR suggestions: Keep a /24 reserved for Redwood runtime services in each AZ (we will provide final list once you confirm AZs). Avoid overlapping with any VPN prefixes used by your partner networks.

3) Packet capture: we'll request tcpdump during the window for one or two flows (we'll provide exact BPF filters). Please confirm storage location and retention for captures (we recommend short retention — 7 days).

If Monday morning is confirmed I'll send a short runbook and the ephemeral key material an hour before the window.

-David
David Kim
Infrastructure Lead, Private Deployments
Redwood Inference



From: Sarah Turner <sarah.turner@nimbushealth.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood-inference.com>
Cc: David Kim <david.kim@redwood-inference.com>, Infra Admin <infra-admin@nimbushealth.com>, Lauren Bishop <lauren.bishop@redwood-inference.com>
Date: Mon, 07 Sep 2026 07:03:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Private deployment — initial network info and questionnaire

Thanks Ava/David —

Monday 09:00-11:00 PT works for us. Infra Admin will provision a temporary jump host and opens the ports listed below. Please note: our compliance team requires that packet captures are stored in the encrypted bucket with access restricted to the joint tech teams. Retention policy will be 7 days as you recommended.

I've attached our updated questionnaire with CIDR ranges filled and the S3 bucket ARN for artifacts. Also included is our internal change ticket ID: CHG-8123.

Attachments: nimbus_private_onboarding_questionnaire.pdf, cicd-auth-flow.png

See you Monday —
Sarah



From: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood-inference.com>
To: Sarah Turner <sarah.turner@nimbushealth.com>, Infra Admin <infra-admin@nimbushealth.com>
Cc: David Kim <david.kim@redwood-inference.com>, Lauren Bishop <lauren.bishop@redwood-inference.com>, Stephanie Nguyen <stephanie.nguyen@redwood-inference.com>
Date: Mon, 07 Sep 2026 15:40:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Private deployment — initial network info and questionnaire

Great — thanks for the confirmation and the CHG ID. David and I will meet the Infra Admin on the jump host at 09:00 PT and run through the wiring checklist. Lauren, please confirm you have the rollout checklist ready and that the test model artifact is available in the provided S3 ARN.

One addition from security: Stephanie, can you validate the KMS key policy example in the wiring guide against Nimbus' policy? We want to ensure key rotation and cross-account decrypt permissions are acceptable before we create the service principal.

title: Implementation compact: sprint play & acceptance pledge
preview:
From: Nikhil Sharma <nikhil@redwood.ai>
To: Sandy Cortez <sandy.cortez@helixai.com>, Diego Ruiz <diego.ruiz@helixai.com>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.ai>, Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>, Monica Patel <monica.patel@redwood.ai>
Date: Thu, 2 Sep 2026 15:02:00 -0700
Subject: Implementation compact: sprint play & acceptance pledge

Hi Sandy / Diego,

As discussed in yesterday's kickoff, attached is a concise "implementation compact" (spreadsheet + one‑page) that pairs milestones with owners, acceptance criteria, and earliest target dates. Goal: provide a single page the execs can read and the delivery team can run against.

High level asks from HelixAI so we can lock dates:

title: Clarify consumption vs first-license surcharge for private VPC deployment
preview:
From: Jordan Ellis <jordan.ellis@mediq.io>
To: Ava Chen <ava@redwood.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa@redwood.com>
Cc: Lina Morales <procurement@mediq.io>
Date: Tue, 01 Sep 2026 09:12:00 -0700
Subject: Pricing follow-up — VPC license & platform fees

Hi Ava / Marissa,

Thanks again for the demo last week. Procurement at Mediq asked for a short write-up about how your pricing works for an on-prem (VPC-hosted) installation — specifically two items: 1) how the first-license "consumption buffer" surcharge works versus ongoing metered consumption, and 2) whether the platform fee is a one-time onboarding surcharge or a recurring monthly charge. They also asked whether we can cap the platform surcharge for the first year.

Could you send a two-line summary that I can drop into our procurement packet? Also attaching the draft SOW we have — we'd like to finalize commercial terms before their Q4 budget approval.

Best,
Jordan
CISO, Mediq Health

Attachment: Mediq_SOW_v2.pdf (application/pdf, 1.1MB)

From: Ava Chen <ava@redwood.com>
To: Jordan Ellis <jordan.ellis@mediq.io>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa@redwood.com>, Sean Gallagher <sean@redwood.com>
Date: Tue, 01 Sep 2026 11:03:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Pricing follow-up — VPC license & platform fees

Jordan — quick response below and I can follow up with more detail. Short summary for procurement: 

- First-license consumption buffer is a one-time provisioning surcharge (covers reserved capacity, image packaging, and a 30-day burst buffer). It is invoiced on signing and capped at the agreed amount in the SOW.
- Platform fee is recurring (monthly) and covers control-plane, observability retention, and patch orchestration; we can provide a discounted ramp in months 1–12 for customers committing to a 12+ month support tier.

If you want a single-line option to paste: "Initial provisioning surcharge (one-time) + recurring platform fee (monthly), with a first-year ramp discount available under a 12-month commitment."

Two quick clarifications to help negotiations: the "consumption buffer" is not usage billing — after the first 30 days, consumption is metered separately (per-token / per-embedding or per-node-hour depending on chosen licensing). Also, platform fee discounts are typically structured as % off for months 1–12 tied to a minimum committed node count.

I can join a call with procurement Thursday if helpful.

— Ava
Director, Sales Engineering
Redwood Inference

Attachment: license_matrix_ava_review.xlsx (spreadsheet, 120KB)

From: Lina Morales <procurement@mediq.io>
To: Ava Chen <ava@redwood.com>, Jordan Ellis <jordan.ellis@mediq.io>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa@redwood.com>, Ethan Rhodes <legal@mediq.io>
Date: Wed, 02 Sep 2026 08:46:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Pricing follow-up — VPC license & platform fees

Thanks Ava — that helps. Procurement asks two follow-ups: (A) can Mediq get a firm cap on total first-year platform spend (provisioning surcharge + 12 months of recurring platform fee) so they can include it in their 2027 capital ask, and (B) how do support tiers map to response times and severity definitions (we need a table to include in the procurement packet). Legal will want any cap reflected as an appendix in the SOW.

Can you provide a capped number range (ballpark okay) and the support-tier bullets by EOD Friday?

Thanks,
Lina
Senior Procurement Manager, Mediq Health

From: Marissa Cole <marissa@redwood.com>
To: Lina Morales <procurement@mediq.io>, Ava Chen <ava@redwood.com>
Cc: Sean Gallagher <sean@redwood.com>, Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>
Date: Thu, 03 Sep 2026 13:15:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Pricing follow-up — VPC license & platform fees

Lina — draft numbers below (ballpark ranges assuming 3-node private pool, Standard support and a modest token profile):

- One-time provisioning surcharge (consumption buffer): $45k — $60k (covers packaging, IP escrow prep, initial 30-day burst).
- Recurring platform fee (monthly): $6k — $9k per month.
- First-year capped spend (worst case): $117k — $168k (surcharge + 12 months platform fee).

Support tiers (summary):
- Standard: Business hours (M–F, 9–5) support, 8–24 hour response depending on Sev; patching windows scheduled monthly.
- Enhanced: 24/7 on-call for Sev1, 4-hour response for Sev1, 8-hour for Sev2, quarterly health checks and 8 hours of migration assistance.
- Enterprise: Dedicated TAM, guaranteed 2-hour Sev1 response, weekly ops reviews, unlimited runbook updates, and 40 hours of onboarding engineering.

Notes: We can convert first-license surcharge to a 12-month amortized credit if Mediq signs a 12+ month committed contract and pre-pays 6 months platform fee; we can include that as an appendix to the SOW.

Happy to convert these ranges into fixed-line items for the SOW appendix once you confirm node count and desired support tier.

— Marissa
Head of Commercials, Redwood Inference

From: Ethan Rhodes <legal@mediq.io>
To: Marissa Cole <marissa@redwood.com>, Ava Chen <ava@redwood.com>, Lina Morales <procurement@mediq.io>
Date: Mon, 06 Sep 2026 16:22:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Pricing follow-up — VPC license & platform fees

Marissa / Ava — Legal reviewed and we like the amortization clause idea. Request two edits for the SOW appendix: (1) explicit definition of "provisioning surcharge" line items (engineering hours, packaging, escrow) with a not-to-exceed figure, and (2) a clear SLA table tying support tiers to response times and escalation contacts.

If you can send a redlined appendix by Wednesday we can fast-track our internal approval. Also confirm whether license transfer on hardware failure is included or priced separately.

Thanks — Ethan
Sr. Counsel, Mediq Health

---
Thread status: action items assigned to Redwood (deliver redlined appendix and license transfer note).

title: Speedgate performance follow-up — A/B plan, metrics we agreed
preview:
From: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.ai>
To: Elena Costa <elena@fastapps.ai>; CloudOps <cloudops@prospectcorp.com>
Cc: Neha Kapoor <neha.kapoor@redwood.ai>
Date: Sat, 05 Sep 2026 09:12:00 -0700
Subject: Speedgate performance follow-up — A/B plan, metrics we agreed

Thanks for the call this morning — quick recap and concrete next steps so we can get the head-to-head tests scheduled. High level: you wanted an apples-to-apples A/B vs your current HyperscaleCloud inference endpoint (HS-API) focused on tail latency and steady-state tokens/sec.

What we covered and agreed:
- Goal metrics (workload: 512-token generative prompts, batch = synthetic mix I sent): p95 <= 180ms, p99 <= 420ms, sustained tokens/sec target >= 400 on a single 80GB GPU for the representative workload.
- Test types: 1) steady-state throughput sweep (tokens/sec), 2) p95/p99 latency under small-burst traffic, 3) cold-start tail characterization.
- Fairness knobs: KV cache disabled for both endpoints, same prompt set, same client-side batching window.
- Deliverables from your side: sample workload (JSON), auth tokens for HS-API test account, and any rate-limiting IP allowlist.

What Redwood will deliver:
- benchmark-config.yaml (attached) — harness config, sampling intervals, percentiles to collect, and pass/fail rules.
- a small helper script and agent to orchestrate runs (we will provide the gist + Docker container).
- a short summary README that maps raw logs to p95/p99 and tokens/sec.

Proposed schedule:
- You share sample-workload.json + credentials by EOD Monday (9/7).
- We spin up comparators and run initial sweep Tue-Wed (9/8–9/9).
- Short readout and decision checklist on Thu (9/10).

Next actions for Elena/CloudOps:
1) Send the workload file and a HS-API test key (or tell us an IP to allowlist).
2) Confirm you’re OK with the p95/p99 thresholds and that we disable caching on both sides.

If that looks right I’ll loop in Rishi to coordinate dedicated infra for the runs and pin a time for the readout.

Thanks,
Ava
Ava Chen
SaaS Account Lead — Redwood Inference
ava.chen@redwood.ai | +1 (415) 555-0198

Attachments: benchmark-config.yaml (attachment stub)


From: Elena Costa <elena@fastapps.ai>
To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.ai>
Cc: CloudOps <cloudops@prospectcorp.com>
Date: Sat, 05 Sep 2026 11:04:00 -04:00
Subject: Re: Speedgate performance follow-up — A/B plan, metrics we agreed

- Harness: benchmark-config.yaml (attached) defines runs at concurrency = [1,4,8,16], tokens-per-request = 512, warmup = 60s, sample window = 120s. We’ll compute p50/p95/p99 and tokens/sec across the sample window and across full run.

From: Neha Kapoor <neha.kapoor@redwood.ai>
To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.ai>; Rishi Malhotra <rishi.malhotra@redwood.ai>
Cc: Elena Costa <elena@fastapps.ai>
Date: Mon, 06 Sep 2026 14:05:00 -0700
Subject: RE: Speedgate performance follow-up — A/B plan, metrics we agreed

Adding a quick tech checklist so the runs are apples-to-apples (copying Elena so she sees infra asks):

- Kernel / runtime parity: ensure we run HS-API with a single-stream mode equivalent to Redwood’s continuous-batching disabled path. We’ll run Redwood with the same kernel selection profile (baseline: attention-optimizations ON, KV cache = OFF).
- Batching window: set to 0ms (no client-side coalescing) for the baseline fairness sweep; we will also run a separate batching-sensitivity experiment later.
- Quantization: do phase-1 with native FP16 paths; record memory footprint so we can suggest quant profiles afterward.
- Observability: collect per-request trace ids, start-to-first-token, end-to-last-token timestamps, CPU/GPU utilization and NVLink metrics if available.
- Artifact locations: upload raw logs to the Drive folder (link above). We’ll run the analyzer which emits a CSV with percentiles and token-throughput computed per-second.

Small note on fallbacks: if HS-API applies automatic fallback to a smaller model under load, please flag that in the run logs — it will invalidate direct comparisons.

I’ll stand up the analyzer and config today and post the helper gist.

-Neha
Neha Kapoor
Senior Perf Engineer — Redwood Inference
neha.kapoor@redwood.ai

- You share sample-workload.json + credentials by EOD Monday (9/7).

title: Invite acceptance blocked by org-binding mismatch (Seastone Logistics)
preview:
From: Elena Ruiz <elena.ruiz@seastone.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Fri, 4 Sep 2026 09:12:00 -0700
Subject: Cannot accept supplier invite — tenant mismatch page

Hi Ben,

We tried to accept the supplier invite you sent this morning but the link lands us on a page that says "Account already claimed / tenant mismatch" and won't let us finish activation. I attached a screenshot of the error page (screenshot-invite-error.png).

We need to get this sorted today — our PO for the initial commit is due next Wednesday and Seastone finance can't receive POs until the vendor record is active. The invite went to maria.ruiz@seastone.com (forwarded internally); I can also try procurement@seastone.com if that helps.

Notes:
- Invite link expired? (we just got it at 08:45)
- We have SSO for other apps; not sure if that is involved here

Can you advise next steps or reissue? If you need any headers/logs I can forward the original invite as an .eml.

Thanks,
Elena Ruiz
Vendor Onboarding Lead
Seastone Logistics
elena.ruiz@seastone.com
+1 (415) 555-0146

Attachment: screenshot-invite-error.png (image/png, 320 KB)

From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwoodinference.com>
To: Elena Ruiz <elena.ruiz@seastone.com>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwoodinference.com>, Priyom Das <priyom.das@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Fri, 4 Sep 2026 10:05:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Cannot accept supplier invite — tenant mismatch page

Hi Elena,

Thanks for the heads-up and for the screenshot — that's very helpful. Short answer: this looks like an org-binding/tenant-affinity mismatch rather than an expired link. Common causes:

- The invite was issued against your company's "Seastone" tenant but the browser is asserting a different corporate identity (forwarded email alias or an existing Seastone guest account on another tenant).
- SSO (SAML) assertion is presenting an entityID that doesn't match the tenant the invite is bound to.

Can you please do two quick things for me so I can triage:
1) Forward the original invite message to me (as an .eml) so I can inspect the tracking token (or paste the invite URL).
2) Try opening the invite link in an incognito window (or from a different browser) to rule out cookie/tenant affinity.

In the meantime I'll open JIRA PRO-4829 to track this and ask Priyom to check the invite binding on our side. If it's faster we can reissue the invite to procure@seastone.com (a shared procurement mailbox) which often avoids forwarded-personal-address problems.

Next steps I'm proposing:
- Priyom: verify the invite token -> tenant mapping and rebind if necessary, or issue a new invite to procure@seastone.com
- Marissa: please approve manual activation if Seastone needs us to bypass automated binding temporarily (we'll record this as an exception).

Elena — thanks for offering the .eml; please forward it when convenient. I'll keep you updated.

Cheers,
Ben Carter
Senior Sales Engineer
Redwood Inference
ben.carter@redwoodinference.com
Attachment: (none)

Related: JIRA PRO-4829


From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwoodinference.com>
To: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwoodinference.com>, Priyom Das <priyom.das@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Fri, 4 Sep 2026 10:12:00 -0700
Subject: Fwd: Cannot accept supplier invite — tenant mismatch page

Marissa / Priyom — forwarding Elena's report. Summary:

- External: Seastone (Elena Ruiz) attempted to accept an invite that seems bound to a different tenant.
- External says the invite went to a forwarded personal address (maria.ruiz@) and they can also receive at procurement@seastone.com.
- Elena attached screenshot and offered to forward original .eml.

Priyom: can you pull the invite token for the invite we sent to maria.ruiz@seastone.com (timestamp 2026-09-04 08:45 PDT) and verify which tenant ID it's bound to? If the token->tenant mapping points to an old org slug, we should rebind to the current Seastone tenant or issue a new invite. If rebind is not safe, issue a fresh invite to procure@seastone.com and include "force-new-account=true" metadata. I've added a sample of the SAML assertion we saw previously (saml-assertion-sample.txt) — it's likely the NameID format that's tripping the check.

Marissa: if Seastone asks for manual activation while we debug, please approve — we should log the exception in PRO-4829.

I'll loop back to Elena once we have either a new invite issued or a tenant rebind.

— Ben

Attachment: saml-assertion-sample.txt (text/plain, 4 KB)

Date: Fri, Sep 4, 2026 at 9:12 AM
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwoodinference.com>
Subject: Cannot accept supplier invite — tenant mismatch page

> We tried to accept the supplier invite you sent this morning but the link lands us on a page that says "Account already claimed / tenant mismatch" and won't let us finish activation. I attached a screenshot of the error page (screenshot-invite-error.png).

> We need to get this sorted today — our PO for the initial commit is due next Wednesday and Seastone finance can't receive POs until the vendor record is active.

> Can you advise next steps or reissue? If you need any headers/logs I can forward the original invite as an .eml.

(please see JIRA PRO-4829 for status and logs)


From: Elena Ruiz <elena.ruiz@seastone.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: IT Ops <itops@seastone.com>
Date: Sun, 6 Sep 2026 11:03:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Cannot accept supplier invite — tenant mismatch page

Hi Ben / team,

Thanks — that did the trick. We got a fresh invite to procurement@seastone.com and were able to finish activation via SSO (incognito test also worked). The portal now shows our vendor record and I can see the pending PO in the Seastone finance queue.

Quick notes in case it helps future triage:
- The original invite had been forwarded internally which resulted in an assertion NameID that mapped to an older Seastone guest account; new invite to the procurement mailbox avoided the conflict.
- Attached I'm forwarding the invite headers that show the forwarded envelope (invite-eml-headers.txt).

Please close out PRO-4829 when you've recorded the root cause and the exception. Appreciate the quick response.

Best,
Elena Ruiz
Vendor Onboarding Lead
Seastone Logistics
